blocks:
Ayahs

صٓ ۚ وَٱلْقُرْءَانِ ذِى ٱلذِّكْرِ (١)
Suad. By the Quran bearing the Reminder, (1)
بَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فِى عِزَّةٍ وَشِقَاقٍ (٢)
the faithless indeed dwell in conceit and defiance. (2)
كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّن قَرْنٍ فَنَادَوا۟ وَّلَاتَ حِينَ مَنَاصٍ (٣)
How many a generation We have destroyed before them! They cried out [for help], but gone was the time for escape. (3)

Circumstances of Revelation

In the books of Tafsir and Hadith, similar circumstances of revelation have been recorded for the opening verses of this Surah. We will mention one of them here, which is more detailed and comprehensive; it is a Hadith narrated by the late Kulayni from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام:

"Abu Jahl and a group from the Quraysh came to Abu Talib, the uncle of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and said: 'Your nephew has annoyed us and has also offended our gods! Call him and command him to stop interfering with our gods so that we, in turn, do not speak ill of His God!' Abu Talib sent someone to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله entered the house and looked around the room, He saw no one but the polytheists alongside Abu Talib. He said:

السلام على من اتبع الهدى‌

'Peace be upon those who follow the guidance!' Then He sat down. Abu Talib explained their words to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied:

او هل لهم فى كلمة خير لهم يسودون بها العرب و يطاون اعناقهم:

'Are they willing to agree with me on a single statement, by virtue of which they may surpass all the Arabs and rule over them?' Abu Jahl (who was thrilled by this statement and expected to receive the key to ruling the Arabs from the Prophet) said: 'Yes, we agree; which statement do You mean?'

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

تقولون لا اله الا اللَّه!:

'Say: There is no deity but Allah!' (And cast away these idols which are the cause of your misery, disgrace, and backwardness).

When those present heard this sentence, they were so terrified that they put their fingers in their ears and rushed out, saying: 'We have never heard such a thing before; this is a fabrication.'

It was at this point that the opening verses of Surah 'Sad' were revealed." [^1]

[^1]: "Usul al-Kafi" (as cited in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 441).

Commentary

The Time for Your Salvation Has Passed

Once again, in the first verse of this Surah, we encounter one of the "Disjointed Letters" (ص - Sad), and the same previous discussions regarding the interpretation of these letters arise: Do they point to the greatness of the Magnificent Quran, which is composed of simple materials like the letters of the alphabet, yet possesses a content that transforms the world of humanity? This is a wondrous manifestation of Allah's power—that He has created such a magnificent composition from those simple materials.

Or do they refer to secrets and codes between Allah and His Prophet, a message from the Intimate to the Intimate?

Or other interpretations.

A group of commentators has specifically emphasized here that "Sad" is an abbreviation for the "Names of Allah" (Asma' Allah) or other terms, as many of Allah's names begin with "Sad," such as "Sadiq" (The Truthful), "Samad" (The Eternal), and "Sani'" (The Maker), or it refers to the phrase "Sadaqallah" (Allah has spoken the truth), summarized in one letter.

For a more detailed explanation regarding the interpretation of the Disjointed Letters, please refer to the beginning of Surahs Al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, and Al-A'raf (in Volumes 1, 2, and 6).

Then He says: "By the Quran containing the reminder, [you are on the truth and this book is a divine miracle]" (وَ الْقُرْآنِ ذِي الذِّكْرِ) [^1]. The Quran is both a "Dhikr" (reminder) itself and "possesses reminder." "Dhikr" means to remind and to wipe away the rust of heedlessness from the tablet of the heart—remembrance of Allah, remembrance of His blessings, remembrance of the Great Court of Resurrection, and remembrance of the purpose of human creation.

Indeed, the major factor in human misery is forgetfulness and heedlessness, and the Magnificent Quran removes it.

Regarding the hypocrites, the Quran says: نَسُوا اللَّهَ فَنَسِيَهُمْ‌: "They forgot Allah, so He forgot them" (and cut off His mercy from them) (At-Tawbah: 67).

And in this very Surah (Sad), verse 26, we read regarding the misguided: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِما نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسابِ‌: "Indeed, those who go astray from the way of Allah will have a severe punishment because they forgot the Day of Account."

Yes, the great affliction of the misguided and the sinners is that very forgetfulness, to the extent that they even forget themselves and their existential values, as the Quran says: وَ لا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ نَسُوا اللَّهَ فَأَنْساهُمْ أَنْفُسَهُمْ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ‌: "And be not like those who forgot Allah, so He made them forget themselves. Those are the defiantly disobedient" (Al-Hashr: 19).

The Quran is a means to tear through these veils of forgetfulness and a light to dispel the darkness of heedlessness. Its verses remind man of Allah and the Hereafter, and its sentences acquaint man with his own existential values.

[^1]: The phrase "وَ الْقُرْآنِ ذِي الذِّكْرِ" is an oath whose response (jawab) is omitted. Its estimated meaning is: "By the Quran containing the reminder, indeed you are truthful and indeed this speech is a miracle."

In the next verse, it says: If you see that they do not submit to these enlightening verses and this awakening Quran, it is not because a veil has fallen over this word of truth, "but the disbelievers are caught in pride and arrogance that prevents them from accepting the truth, and in enmity and rebellion that hinders them from accepting your call" (بَلِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي عِزَّةٍ وَ شِقاقٍ‌).

"Izzah," according to Raghib in "Al-Mufradat," is a state that prevents a person from being overcome (a state of invincibility), and it is originally derived from "Azaz," meaning hard, firm, and impenetrable ground... It is of two types: sometimes it is "praiseworthy Izzah" (honor), as we describe the Pure Essence of Allah as "Aziz" (The Almighty), and sometimes it is "blameworthy Izzah," which is impenetrability against the truth and arrogance in accepting realities—and this "Izzah" is, in reality, humiliation! "Shiqaq" is from the root "Shaqq," originally meaning a crack or split; it is then used to mean discord or opposition, because discord causes each group to be in a separate "Shaqq" (side/fringe).

The Quran here considers the issue of impenetrability, pride, arrogance, and following the path of separation and discord as the cause of the disbelievers' misery. Indeed, these are ugly and ominous traits that cast a veil over a person's eyes and ears and take away the sense of discernment. How painful it is for the eyes to be open and the ears to be open, yet the person remains blind and deaf?

In verse 206 of Surah Al-Baqarah, we read: وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُ اتَّقِ اللَّهَ أَخَذَتْهُ الْعِزَّةُ بِالْإِثْمِ فَحَسْبُهُ جَهَنَّمُ وَ لَبِئْسَ الْمِهادُ: "And when it is said to him (the hypocrite), 'Fear Allah,' pride in the sin takes hold of him. So Hell is sufficient for him, and how wretched is the resting place."

Then, to awaken these heedless arrogant ones, He takes them by the hand and leads them through the history of mankind, showing them the fate of arrogant, proud, and stubborn nations, so that perhaps they may take heed. He says: "How many a generation have We destroyed before them (for denying the prophets, rejecting divine verses, and committing oppression and sin)?" (كَمْ أَهْلَكْنا مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنْ قَرْنٍ‌).

"And when the punishment descended, they cried out for help, but what use was it, for it was too late, and the time for escape had passed" (فَنادَوْا وَ لاتَ حِينَ مَناصٍ‌).

On the day when the divine prophets and the guardians of truth exhorted them and warned them of the ominous consequences of their actions, they not only lacked a listening ear but also resorted to mockery, ridicule, and the harassment of believers, and even their murder. Opportunities were lost, the bridges behind them were destroyed, and the "punishment of eradication" (Adhab al-Istisal) descended to destroy them at a time when the doors of repentance and return were all closed, and their cries for help reached nowhere!

The word "Lat" is for negation and was originally "La" (the negative particle), with the "feminine Ta" added to it for emphasis. [^1] "Manas" is from the root "Naws," meaning a place of refuge or a helper. It is said that when a severe and terrifying event occurred, especially in wars, the Arabs would repeat this word, saying "Manas, Manas," meaning "Where is the refuge? Where is the refuge?" And because this concept is associated with fleeing, it has sometimes been used to mean a place of escape. [^2] In any case, these arrogant heedless ones did not take advantage of the opportunity to seek refuge in the loving embrace of Allah's grace while they had the chance; and when the opportunities were lost and the punishment of eradication descended, these cries for help and efforts to find a way of escape and refuge lead nowhere.

This has been the Sunnah (way) of the Lord with all previous nations, and it will continue in the future, for there is no change or alteration in His Sunnah.

Alas, many people are not willing to use the experiences of others; they must experience the bitter trials themselves once again—trials that sometimes occur only once in a person's lifetime, with no second chance, where the beginning and the end are, so to speak, one and the same.

[^1]: Some have also considered the "Ta" to be "superfluous" (Zaidah) and for hyperbole (like Allamah), just as some have considered "La" here to be "La of categorical negation" (Nafiyah lil-Jins), and others as "resembling Laysa." In any case, after adding the "Ta" to it, it acquires specific rules, including that it is strictly used regarding time, and that either its subject (Ism) or its predicate (Khabar) is always omitted, with only one of the two mentioned in the speech. Therefore, the phrase "وَ لاتَ حِينَ مَناصٍ‌" was originally "و لات الحين حين مناص" (and the time was not a time of escape).

[^2]: Mufradat al-Raghib, Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, and the book Majma' al-Bahrayn under the root "Naws."

Ayahs

وَعَجِبُوٓا۟ أَن جَآءَهُم مُّنذِرٌ مِّنْهُمْ ۖ وَقَالَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ هَـٰذَا سَـٰحِرٌ كَذَّابٌ (٤)
They consider it odd that there should come to them a warner from among themselves, and the faithless say, ‘This is a magician, a mendacious liar.’ (4)
أَجَعَلَ ٱلْـَٔالِهَةَ إِلَـٰهًا وَٰحِدًا ۖ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَشَىْءٌ عُجَابٌ (٥)
‘Has he reduced the gods to one god? This is indeed an odd thing!’ (5)
وَٱنطَلَقَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِنْهُمْ أَنِ ٱمْشُوا۟ وَٱصْبِرُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ ءَالِهَتِكُمْ ۖ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَشَىْءٌ يُرَادُ (٦)
Their elite go about [urging others]: ‘Go and stand by your gods! This is indeed the desirable thing [to do]. (6)
مَا سَمِعْنَا بِهَـٰذَا فِى ٱلْمِلَّةِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا ٱخْتِلَـٰقٌ (٧)
We did not hear of this in the latter-day creed. This is nothing but a fabrication. (7)

Circumstances of Revelation

Regarding these verses, a circumstance of revelation similar to what was mentioned for the previous verses has been narrated, and it is not unlikely that there is a single occasion of revelation for this entire set of verses.

However, since this narration contains new details, we present it here from the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, as follows:

When the Messenger of Allah revealed his mission publicly, the leaders of the Quraysh came to Abu Talib and said: "O Abu Talib! Your nephew calls us light-headed, insults our gods, has corrupted our youth, and has sown division among our community. If these actions are due to financial need, we will gather so much wealth for him that he becomes the richest man of the Quraysh, and we are even ready to choose him as our leader."

Abu Talib conveyed this message to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. The Prophet ﷺ replied:

«لو وضعوا الشمس فى يمينى و القمر فى يسارى ما اردته، و لكن كلمة يعطونى يملكون بها العرب و تدين بها العجم و يكونون ملوكا فى الجنة!»:

"If they were to place the sun in my right hand and the moon in my left, I would not desire it. However, (instead of all these promises) let them agree with me on one sentence, by which they shall rule over the Arabs, and the non-Arabs shall submit to their faith, and they shall be kings in Paradise!"

Abu Talib conveyed this message to them. They said: "We are ready to accept ten sentences instead of one (which sentence do you mean?)."

The Prophet ﷺ said to them:

«تشهدون ان لا اله الا اللَّه و انى رسول اللَّه»:

"Bear witness that there is no deity but Allah and that I am the Messenger of Allah."

They (were deeply terrified by this statement and) said: "Should we abandon 360 gods to go after only one God? What a strange thing?! (And that too, a God who is never seen!)."

At this point, the following verses were revealed: "And they wonder that a warner has come to them from among themselves. And the disbelievers say, 'This is a magician and a liar.' ... This is nothing but a fabrication." [1] The same meaning is narrated in Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan with slight differences, and at its end, it states: "The Prophet ﷺ, while tears were flowing from his eyes, said: 'O uncle! If they place the sun in my right hand and the moon in my left so that I abandon this word, I will never do so, unless I make this word prevail in society, or I am killed in its path.' When Abu Talib heard this, he said: 'Go forth with your mission; by Allah, I will never cease to support you.'" [2]

Commentary

Should we accept one God instead of all these gods?!

Arrogant and self-centered individuals are both impenetrable and "absolutist"; they recognize nothing except what they have perceived with their limited and deficient thoughts, and they make that the criterion for measuring all values.

Therefore, when the Prophet of Islam ﷺ raised the banner of Monotheism (Tawhid) in Mecca and rose against the small and large idols—which numbered up to 360—they sometimes "wondered why a warner from among themselves had arisen?"

(وَ عَجِبُوا أَنْ جاءَهُمْ مُنْذِرٌ مِنْهُمْ)

Their wonder was because Muhammad ﷺ was one of them. Why had an angel not descended from heaven? They perceived this great point of strength as a point of weakness. Someone who had risen from among the masses of the people was aware of their needs and pains, familiar with the problems and issues of their lives, and could be a model and example in everything. They regarded this great advantage as a dark spot in the Prophet's ﷺ call and were astonished by it.

Sometimes they went even further than this stage: "And the disbelievers said, 'This is a magician and a liar!'" (وَ قالَ الْكافِرُونَ هذا ساحِرٌ كَذَّابٌ).

We have repeatedly stated that attributing magic to the Prophet ﷺ was due to witnessing his undeniable miracles and his extraordinary influence on minds. Attributing lies to him was because he rose against the superstitious traditions and degraded thoughts that were considered certainties in that environment, spoke against them, and claimed a mission from Allah.

When the Prophet revealed his monotheistic call, they looked at one another and said: "Come and hear unheard-of things! Has he made the gods into one God? Indeed, this is a curious thing!" (أَ جَعَلَ الْآلِهَةَ إِلهاً واحِداً إِنَّ هذا لَشَيْ‌ءٌ عُجابٌ) [1]. Yes, sometimes pride, selfishness, absolutism, and the corruption of the environment so alter a person's insight and judgment that they wonder at clear realities while remaining strictly committed to superstitions and hollow fancies. The word "Ujab" (عُجاب), like "Tuwal" (طوال), conveys an intensive meaning and is used for things that are extremely strange.

These light-headed people thought that the more the number of their deities, the greater their power and influence would be. For this reason, the One God seemed like something small to them. Meanwhile, we know that from a philosophical perspective, multiple things are always limited, and an unlimited existence cannot be more than one. For this reason, all studies in theology lead to the line of "Monotheism."

"Their leaders, when they became hopeless and despaired of appealing to Abu Talib and his mediation, left his presence and said: 'Go and cling firmly to your gods, and show steadfastness and endurance, for Muhammad's ﷺ goal is to lead our society to corruption and ruin, to cut off Allah's blessings from us because of turning our backs on the idols, and to rule over us himself!'" (وَ انْطَلَقَ الْمَلَأُ مِنْهُمْ أَنِ امْشُوا وَ اصْبِرُوا عَلى‌ آلِهَتِكُمْ إِنَّ هذا لَشَيْ‌ءٌ يُرادُ).

"Intalaqa" (انطلق) comes from the root "Inthilaq," meaning to depart quickly while abandoning a previous task. Here, it refers to leaving Abu Talib's assembly in anger and rage.

"Mala'" (ملا) refers to the nobles and prominent figures of the Quraysh who came to Abu Talib. After leaving that assembly, they said to one another or to their followers: "Do not abandon the idols and cling firmly to your deities."

The phrase "لَشَيْ‌ءٌ يُرادُ" (is a thing intended) means "this matter is something desired." Since it is a concise phrase, commentators have mentioned many interpretations for it, including:

Some have said: It refers to the call of the Noble Prophet of Islam, meaning that this call is a conspiracy whose target is us. It has an outward appearance of calling towards Allah, but an inward goal of ruling over us and achieving sovereignty and leadership over the Arabs, and all these are just excuses for this objective. You people, go and stand firm in your religion, and leave the analysis of this conspiracy to us, the leaders of the tribe!

This is something that the leaders of falsehood have always proposed to silence the voice of the followers of the path of truth. They called it a conspiracy—a conspiracy that politicians must analyze carefully and plan a program to combat, while the masses should pass by it indifferently and cling tightly to what they have! A similar statement appears in the story of Noah, where the nobles and leaders said to the masses: "This is but a man like yourselves who wishes to assert superiority over you" (Al-Mu'minun: 24).

Others have said in interpreting this phrase: It means that you idolaters should stand firm regarding your gods; this is exactly what is expected of you.

Some have also said: It means Muhammad's ﷺ target is us; he wants to lead our society to corruption and make us turn our backs on our gods, resulting in blessings being cut off from us and punishment descending upon us!

Some have also suggested: It means that Muhammad ﷺ will not give up his work; it is a decision already made and an irrevocable will. Therefore, negotiating with him is useless; go and keep your beliefs firm.

And finally, it has been suggested that they meant: This is a calamity that has befallen us, and in any case, we must endure it and keep our religion firm.

Of course, given the general nature of this phrase's concept, most of these interpretations may be combined within it, although the first meaning seems most appropriate of all.

In any case, the leaders of the idolaters wanted to use these words to strengthen the wavering morale of their followers and prevent their beliefs from collapsing further. But what a futile effort!

Then, to deceive the people or to convince themselves, they said: "We never heard of such a thing from our forefathers; this is nothing but a lie and a fabrication!" (ما سَمِعْنا بِهذا فِي الْمِلَّةِ الْآخِرَةِ إِنْ هذا إِلَّا اخْتِلاقٌ).

If the claim of Monotheism and the rejection of idols were a reality, our forefathers, with all their greatness and character, should have understood it, and we should have heard it from them. But this is a false and unprecedented statement! The expression "the latter religion" (الْمِلَّةِ الْآخِرَةِ) might refer to the generation of their fathers, who were the "last nation" relative to them, as mentioned above. It might also refer to the "People of the Book," especially the "Christians," who were considered the last religion and nation before the appearance of the Prophet of Islam ﷺ. That is, there is no trace of Muhammad's ﷺ words in the books of the Christians either, because they believe in the "Trinity" (three gods); Muhammad's ﷺ Monotheism is a novel matter! However, as the tone of the Quran in various other verses shows, the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance did not rely on the books of the Jews and Christians; their entire reliance was on the tradition and religion of their ancestors and fathers, and this is a good evidence for the first interpretation.

"Ikhtilaq" (اختلاق) comes from the root "Khalq," which originally means to initiate something without precedent. Later, this word was also applied to "lying," because a liar often presents unprecedented matters. Therefore, the meaning of "Ikhtilaq" in the verse under discussion is that the claim of Monotheism is a novel and unprecedented claim that Muhammad ﷺ has proposed, and it was completely unknown among us and our predecessors, and this itself is proof of its falsehood!

Key Point: Fear of Innovation!

Fear of new and novel issues has been one of the reasons throughout history for the insistence of misguided peoples on their deviations and their refusal to submit to the call of the divine prophets. They were terrified of anything new, and for this reason, they looked upon the religion of the prophets with extreme pessimism. Traces of this ignorant thinking still exist in many peoples. This is while neither the prophets' call to Monotheism was a new matter, nor, if something were new, would it be proof of its falsehood. One must follow logic and submit to the truth, wherever it may be and from whomever it may come.

It is strange that the fear of innovation sometimes, unfortunately, also affects some scholars, and they raise the flag of opposition against new scientific views and say, "This is nothing but a fabrication!" This issue is seen particularly often in the history of the Church authorities, who rose against the scientific discoveries of natural scientists and targeted people like Galileo with the harshest attacks for discovering the earth's movement around the sun and itself, saying: "These words are heresy and an unprecedented lie!"

What is even stranger is that some great figures, when they reached new scientific innovations, would struggle to find a few among the ancients and predecessors who agreed with their new views, out of fear that they might be attacked for innovation by those who criticized them due to the "veil of contemporaneity"! In this way, they would present their view as an old and ancient belief to remain safe, and this is very painful! An example of this can be seen regarding the famous theory of "Substantial Motion" (al-harakat al-jawhariyya) by Sadr al-Muta'allihin Shirazi in Al-Asfar.

In any case, this way of dealing with new issues and innovations has had and continues to have great losses for human societies and for the world of science and knowledge. Sincere enthusiasts must strive to reform it and cleanse these ignorant residues from minds.

However, this does not mean that we should welcome every new matter just because it is new, even if it is baseless and without foundation, for "neophilia" (obsession with the new), like "love for the old," is itself a great affliction.

Islamic moderation requires that there be neither that "excess" nor this "deficiency."

[^1]: Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 442 (Hadith 7). [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 8, p. 65. [^1]: The meaning of "Jal" (making) here is not ontological creation, but rather establishing it according to belief.

Ayahs

أَءُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ ٱلذِّكْرُ مِنۢ بَيْنِنَا ۚ بَلْ هُمْ فِى شَكٍّ مِّن ذِكْرِى ۖ بَل لَّمَّا يَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابِ (٨)
Has the Reminder been sent down to him out of [all of] us?’ Indeed, they are in doubt concerning My Reminder. Indeed, they have not yet tasted My punishment. (8)
أَمْ عِندَهُمْ خَزَآئِنُ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّكَ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْوَهَّابِ (٩)
Do they possess the treasuries of the mercy of your Lord, the All-mighty, the All-munificent? (9)
أَمْ لَهُم مُّلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ۖ فَلْيَرْتَقُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَسْبَـٰبِ (١٠)
Do they own the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them? [If so,] let them ascend [to the higher spheres] by the means [of ascension]. (10)
جُندٌ مَّا هُنَالِكَ مَهْزُومٌ مِّنَ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ (١١)
[They are but] a host routed out there of the factions. (11)

Commentary

This Small, Defeated Army!

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on the negative stance of the opponents against the path of Monotheism and the mission of the Prophet of Islam. This discourse continues in the verses under discussion.

When the polytheists of Mecca saw their illegitimate interests in danger, and the fire of malice and envy flared up in their hearts, they resorted to various weak logics to deceive the people and justify their opposition to the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family). Among these, they said out of wonder and denial: "Has the Quran been revealed only to Muhammad among all of us?" (أَ أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ مِنْ بَيْنِنا).

Among all these aged elders, all these wealthy and prominent figures, was there no one found for God to reveal His Quran to, except Muhammad, the poor orphan?! This logic was not exclusive to that time; in every age and era, whenever an important responsibility is entrusted to someone, the spirit of envy flares up, eyes stare, ears sharpen, and grumbling and excuse-making begin. They say: "Could no one else be found that this task was given to so-and-so, who comes from an obscure and poor family?"

Indeed, worldliness on one hand and envy on the other caused the People of the Book (Jews and Christians), who shared common ground with the Muslims, to distance themselves from Islam and the Quran and turn toward the idolaters, saying that their way is better than the way of the believers:

أَ لَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا نَصِيباً مِنَ الْكِتابِ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْجِبْتِ وَ الطَّاغُوتِ وَ يَقُولُونَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا هؤُلاءِ أَهْدى‌ مِنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا سَبِيلًا: "Have you not seen those who were given a portion of the Book? They believe in Jibt and Taghut (idols and false deities) and say to the disbelievers, 'These are better guided than those who believe'" (An-Nisa, 51).

It is obvious that these expressions of wonder and denial—which, besides envy and love for the world, had another source, namely a mistake in "recognizing values"—could never be a logical criterion for judgment. Is a person's character defined by their name, fame, money, status, or age? Is Divine mercy distributed based on these criteria?

Therefore, in the continuation of the verse, He states that their pain is something else: "In fact, they are in doubt regarding My Revelation and Reminder" (بَلْ هُمْ فِي شَكٍّ مِنْ ذِكْرِي‌).

The objection to the person of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family) is nothing more than an excuse. Their doubt in this matter is not due to any ambiguity in the Glorious Quran, but rather its source is whims, desires, love for the world, and envy.

Finally, He threatens them with this sentence: "They have not yet tasted Divine punishment [to act so boldly against God's messenger and rise to war against Divine revelation with such vain words]" (بَلْ لَمَّا يَذُوقُوا عَذابِ‌). Indeed, there is always a group whose ears are closed to logic and sound reasoning, and nothing but the whips of punishment will bring them down from their steed of arrogance. They must be punished, for their only cure is Divine chastisement.

Then, in response to them, He adds: "Or do they possess the treasuries of the mercy of your Lord, the All-Mighty, the Bestower?" so that they may grant the decree of prophethood to whomever they wish and deprive whomever they dislike? (أَمْ عِنْدَهُمْ خَزائِنُ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّكَ الْعَزِیزِ الْوَهَّابِ‌).

By virtue of being the "Lord" (Rabb)—the Owner and Nurturer of the universe and humanity—Allah chooses someone for His mission who can lead the people on the path of perfection and education. By virtue of being "All-Mighty" (Aziz), He is not overcome by anyone's desires such that He would entrust the station of prophethood to an unworthy individual. Fundamentally, the station of prophethood is so grand that only Allah has the power to grant it. And by virtue of being the "Bestower" (Wahhab), He grants whatever He wills to whomever He deems fit.

It is noteworthy that "Wahhab" is an intensive form meaning "The Frequent Bestower," indicating that prophethood is not just a single gift, but multiple gifts that come together so that someone can undertake that office: the gifts of knowledge, piety, infallibility, courage, and bravery.

We read a similar statement in Surah Az-Zukhruf, verse 32: أَ هُمْ يَقْسِمُونَ رَحْمَتَ رَبِّكَ‌: "Do they distribute the mercy of your Lord?" [objecting to the Quran being revealed to you].

Furthermore, from the use of the term "mercy," it is clearly understood that prophethood is a mercy and grace from Allah upon humanity. Truly it is so, for if there were no prophets, humans would lose both the way to the Hereafter and spirituality, as well as the way of this world—just as those who have distanced themselves from the school of the prophets have lost both paths.

Again, in the next verse, He pursues the same meaning through another route, saying: "Or is the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them theirs? If so, let them ascend by any means [to the heavens] and prevent the descent of Divine revelation upon the pure heart of Muhammad!" (أَمْ لَهُمْ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما فَلْيَرْتَقُوا فِي الْأَسْبابِ‌).

This statement is actually a completion of the previous discussion. There, it said: "The treasuries of your Lord's mercy are not in your hands to bestow upon whoever aligns with your lustful desires." Now it says: since these treasuries are not in your hands and are only at Allah's disposal, the only way left for you is to go to the heavens and prevent the descent of His revelation—and you know well that you are utterly incapable of this! Therefore, neither is the "requirement" in your hands, nor do you have the power to create an "obstacle." Given this, what can you possibly achieve? Die in your envy, and do whatever you can.

In this way, these two verses do not repeat the same point—as some commentators have suggested—but rather each looks at one dimension of the issue.

In the final verse under discussion, in order to belittle these shallow-minded and boastful arrogant ones, He says: "[They are but] a small, defeated army of the [many] factions!" (جُنْدٌ ما هُنالِكَ مَهْزُومٌ مِنَ الْأَحْزابِ‌) [1]. "Hunalika" means "there" and is used to point to something distant. For this reason, some consider it an allusion to the defeat of the polytheists in the Battle of Badr, which took place at a point relatively distant from Mecca.

The term "Ahzab" (factions) apparently refers to all the groups that rose up against the prophets and whom Allah crushed. This gathering of polytheists is but a small faction among those groups and will meet the same fate. (The evidence for this is the upcoming verses which explicitly mention this issue).

Let us not forget that this Surah is among the Meccan Surahs, and the Quran spoke these words at a time when the Muslims were in a severe minority, such that the polytheists could have snatched them away like a single morsel: "fearing that the people might snatch you away" (Al-Anfal, 26).

On that day, no sign of victory was visible for the Muslims. The victories of Badr, Al-Ahzab, and Hunayn had not yet occurred, yet the Quran said with decisiveness: "These stubborn enemies are a small army that will suffer defeat."

Today, the Quran gives this same glad tidings to the Muslims of the world who are surrounded on all sides by aggressive and oppressive powers: that if they stand by Allah's covenant like the early Muslims, He too will fulfill His promise regarding the defeat of the "armies of the factions."

[1]: The word "ma" in the above phrase is an augmentative particle used for diminution (to show insignificance), and "jund" is the predicate of a suppressed subject, and "mahzum" is a second predicate. Originally it was: "They are a small, defeated army from the factions." Some believe there is no suppressed subject and "jund" is the subject while "mahzum" is the predicate, but the first view seems more appropriate.

Ayahs

كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَعَادٌ وَفِرْعَوْنُ ذُو ٱلْأَوْتَادِ (١٢)
Before them Noah’s people impugned [their apostle] and [so did the people of] ‘Ad, and Pharaoh, the Impaler [of his victims], (12)
وَثَمُودُ وَقَوْمُ لُوطٍ وَأَصْحَـٰبُ لْـَٔيْكَةِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلْأَحْزَابُ (١٣)
and Thamud, and the people of Lot, and the inhabitants of Aykah: those were the factions. (13)
إِن كُلٌّ إِلَّا كَذَّبَ ٱلرُّسُلَ فَحَقَّ عِقَابِ (١٤)
There was not any one but such as impugned the apostles; so My retribution became due [against them]. (14)
وَمَا يَنظُرُ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً مَّا لَهَا مِن فَوَاقٍ (١٥)
These [too] do not await but a single Cry, which will not grant [them] any respite. (15)
وَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا عَجِّل لَّنَا قِطَّنَا قَبْلَ يَوْمِ ٱلْحِسَابِ (١٦)
They say, ‘Our Lord! Hasten on for us our share before the Day of Reckoning.’ (16)

Commentary

Only a Single Heavenly Blast Will Finish Them!

Following the previous verse which foretold the future defeat of the polytheists and described them as a small army among the defeated factions, the verses under discussion introduce a group of these "factions" (Ahzab) who denied the Prophets and met a grim fate.

It says: "Before them, the people of Noah, ‘Ad, and Pharaoh, the lord of stakes (possessor of power), denied the divine signs and His messengers" (كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَ عادٌ وَ فِرْعَوْنُ ذُو الْأَوْتادِ).

"Likewise, the people of Thamud, the people of Lot, and the Dwellers of the Wooded Elm (the people of Shu’ayb); these were the factions who rose to deny the Prophets" (وَ ثَمُودُ وَ قَوْمُ لُوطٍ وَ أَصْحابُ الْأَيْكَةِ أُولئِكَ الْأَحْزابُ) [1]. Indeed, these were six groups from the ignorant and idol-worshipping factions who rose against great Prophets:

The people of Noah against this magnificent Prophet. The people of ‘Ad against Prophet Hud (عليه السلام). Pharaoh against Moses (عليه السلام) and Aaron (عليه السلام). The people of Thamud against Salih (عليه السلام). The people of Lot against Prophet Lot (عليه السلام). And the Dwellers of the Wooded Elm against Shu’ayb (عليه السلام).

They employed everything in their power to deny, harass, and torment the Prophets and the believers, but ultimately, divine punishment seized them and harvested them like a withered field.

[1]: The phrase "أُولئِكَ الْأَحْزابُ" consists of a subject and a predicate. "أُولئِكَ" (those) refers to the six nations mentioned in these two verses, and "الْأَحْزابُ" (the factions) refers to the same factions mentioned two verses prior, where the polytheists of Mecca were considered a small group among them.

The people of Noah were destroyed by the flood and torrential rains. ‘Ad by a fierce and crushing gale. Pharaoh and his folk by the waves of the Nile. The people of Thamud by a heavenly blast (a mighty thunderbolt). The people of Lot by a terrifying earthquake accompanied by a rain of stones from the sky. The people of Shu’ayb by a lethal thunderbolt that descended upon them from a cloud.

In this manner, "water," "wind," "earth," and "fire"—which constitute the primary means of human life—were commissioned to bring about their death. They folded the scroll of the lives of these rebellious tyrants so thoroughly that no trace of them remained.

The polytheists of Mecca should reflect: compared to these nations, they are nothing more than a small group. Why do they not wake up from the sleep of negligence?

The description of Pharaoh as "ذُو الْأَوْتادِ" (Lord of the Stakes/Firm Pegs), which appears more explicitly in these verses and in verse 10 of Surah Al-Fajr, is a metaphor for the stability of the power of Pharaoh and his people. This expression is also used in everyday speech to mean stability; it is said: "So-and-so's pegs are firm," or "the pegs of this matter have been driven in," or "it has been four-pegged," because various types of pegs are always used to stabilize a building or a tent.

Some have also considered it a reference to Pharaoh's massive armies, as an army usually uses tents, and pegs are used to hold the tents.

Others have considered it a reference to the horrific tortures the Pharaohs inflicted upon their enemies, known as "four-pegging" them, where each of their hands and feet was nailed with a stake to the ground, a gallows, or a wall, and they were left to die! Finally, some have suggested that "Awtad" (stakes/pegs) refers to the Pyramids of Egypt, which sit in the heart of the earth like pegs; since the Pyramids are a unique characteristic of the Pharaohs, this description in the Quran appears exclusively regarding them.

At the same time, these possibilities are not mutually exclusive, and the concept of the word may encompass all of them.

Regarding the "Dwellers of the Wooded Elm" (Ashab al-Aykah), "Aykah" means a tree, and they are the people of Prophet Shu’ayb (عليه السلام) who lived in a land rich in water and trees between the Hijaz and Levant. We have discussed this sufficiently in the commentary of Surah Al-Hijr under verse 78 (Volume 11, page 120).

"Indeed, every one of these groups denied the messengers of the Lord, and thus the divine punishment was realized upon them" (إِنْ كُلٌّ إِلَّا كَذَّبَ الرُّسُلَ فَحَقَّ عِقابِ) [1]. History shows how each group perished by a calamity, and in a short time, their cities and lands turned into ruins, and their individuals into soulless corpses!

Can these polytheists of Mecca, with these actions of theirs, expect a better fate than them? While their deeds are the same deeds and the Sunnah (way) of Allah is the same Sunnah?!

[1]: The phrase "فَحَقَّ عِقابِ" was originally "فحق عقابى" (My punishment became due). According to common usage, the "Ya" was omitted, and the Kasra indicating it remains. "Haqqa" is the verb and "Iqab" is its subject, meaning: "My punishment became established and realized regarding them."

Therefore, in the next verse, as a decisive and crushing threat, He says: "These people, with these deeds, await nothing but a single heavenly blast, a blast from which there is no return" (وَ ما يَنْظُرُ هؤُلاءِ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً واحِدَةً ما لَها مِنْ فَواقٍ).

This blast might be similar to the blasts that descended upon previous nations—a terrifying thunderbolt or a loud earthquake that crushed their lives.

It might also refer to the "Great Blast of the End of the World," which is described as the "First Blowing of the Trumpet."

Some commentators have raised objections to the first interpretation, considering it contrary to verse 33 of Surah Al-Anfal, which states: "But Allah would not punish them while you are among them."

However, considering that the polytheists did not hold this belief regarding the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), and their deeds were identical to the deeds of the nations who perished by heavenly blasts, they should expect such a fate at any moment, for the verse speaks of the issue of "awaiting" (reflect on this).

Some have also objected to the second interpretation, arguing that the Arab polytheists will not be alive at the end of the world for that great blast to seize them.

But this objection is also invalid for the same reason we mentioned before: no one knows the moment of the end of the world and the onset of the Resurrection. Therefore, on that day, the polytheists should expect that great and irreversible blast at any moment [1].

[1]: As for the possibility suggested by some commentators that it refers to the "Second Blast" which occurs at the time of the resurrection of the dead and their presence in the court of divine justice, it seems very unlikely, as it is consistent neither with the following verse nor the preceding ones (reflect on this).

In any case, these heedless ones, by denying and rejecting the divine signs, making false accusations against the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), and persisting in idolatry, injustice, and corruption, are as if they are sitting in wait for divine punishment—a punishment that would burn the harvest of their lives, or a blast that would end the life of the world and take them on a path of no return.

"Fawaq" (rhyming with Rawaq), as stated by many linguists and commentators, originally means the interval between two milkings of a camel's udder; because when the milk is completely drawn, one must wait a little for the milk to collect in the udder again.

Some define it as the interval between opening and closing the fingers while milking with the fingers.

Since the udder enters a state of rest after milking, this word is sometimes used to mean rest and tranquility.

Also, since this interval is for the milk to return to the udder, this expression has been used to mean return and recourse. For this reason, the recovery of a patient is called "Afaqah," because health and wellness return to him. Similarly, the regaining of consciousness by a drunkard or the return of sanity to a madman is called "Afaqah" because of the return of consciousness and intellect to them [1]. In any case, this terrifying blast has no return, no rest, no tranquility, and no stillness. When it is realized, all doors are closed to man; neither regret is of any use, nor is there a possibility of compensation, nor do cries reach anywhere.

[1]: Some linguists have made distinctions between "Fawaq" (with Fatha) and "Fuwaq" (with Damma), while others consider both to have the same meaning. For further explanation, refer to Raghib's Mufradat, Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Fakhr Razi, Tafsir Abu al-Futuh, Qurtubi, and linguistic sources.

The last verse under discussion refers to another statement of the disbelievers and deniers, which they said out of mockery and derision: "And they said: Our Lord! Hasten for us our share of the punishment before the Day of Account!" (وَ قالُوا رَبَّنا عَجِّلْ لَنا قِطَّنا قَبْلَ يَوْمِ الْحِسابِ).

These arrogant, blind-hearted ones were so intoxicated with the wine of pride that they even mocked divine punishment and His court of justice, saying: "Why is our quota of punishment delayed? Why does God not give us our share sooner?!" Among previous nations, such self-satisfied, light-headed people were not few, but at the moment of being caught in the clutches of divine punishment, they roared like animals, and no one came to their aid.

"Qitt" (rhyming with Jinn) originally means something that is cut widthwise, and "Qadd" (of the same rhyme) means something cut lengthwise! Since the specific portion and quota of each person is like something "cut off" and designated, this word is also used to mean "share."

Sometimes it refers to a paper on which something is written, or where the names of individuals and their prizes are recorded.

Therefore, some commentators, in interpreting the above verse, have said it means: "O Allah, give us our book of deeds before the Day of Recompense." They said this when the verses of the Quran informed that on the Day of Resurrection, a group will have their book of deeds in their right hand and a group in their left.

Out of mockery, they said: "How good it would be if our book of deeds were given to us now? So that we may read it and see what we have done."

In any case, "ignorance" and "arrogance" are two very ugly and reprehensible traits that are often inseparable; the ignorant are arrogant, and the arrogant are ignorant. The effects of these two were abundantly visible in the polytheists of the Age of Ignorance.

Ayahs

ٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ وَٱذْكُرْ عَبْدَنَا دَاوُۥدَ ذَا ٱلْأَيْدِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥٓ أَوَّابٌ (١٧)
Be patient over what they say, and remember Our servant, David, [the man] of strength. Indeed he was a penitent [soul]. (17)
إِنَّا سَخَّرْنَا ٱلْجِبَالَ مَعَهُۥ يُسَبِّحْنَ بِٱلْعَشِىِّ وَٱلْإِشْرَاقِ (١٨)
We disposed the mountains to glorify [Allah] with him at evening and dawn, (18)
وَٱلطَّيْرَ مَحْشُورَةً ۖ كُلٌّ لَّهُۥٓ أَوَّابٌ (١٩)
and the birds [as well], mustered [in flocks]; all echoing him [in a chorus]. (19)
وَشَدَدْنَا مُلْكَهُۥ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَفَصْلَ ٱلْخِطَابِ (٢٠)
We consolidated his kingdom and gave him wisdom and conclusive speech. (20)

Commentary

Learn a Lesson from the Life of Dawud

"Dawud" (David) was one of the great prophets of the Children of Israel who possessed a vast kingdom. His lofty station is praised in numerous verses of the Holy Quran. Following the discussions in the previous verses regarding the obstructions caused by the polytheists and idolaters, and their unjust accusations against the Prophet of Islam ص، the Quran introduces the story of Dawud here to comfort the Prophet ص and the few believers of that time. Dawud was a man to whom Allah granted such immense power that He even made the mountains and the birds subservient to him, demonstrating that when His grace encompasses someone, the masses of enemies can achieve nothing.

However, even this great Prophet, despite all his outward power, was not safe from the stinging tongues of the people. This serves as a consolation for the Prophet ص, showing that this issue is not unique to him and that the great figures of the world have shared in this experience.

First, it says: "Be patient over what they say and remember Our servant, Dawud, the possessor of strength; indeed, he was one who repeatedly turned [to Allah]" (اصْبِرْ عَلى‌ ما يَقُولُونَ وَ اذْكُرْ عَبْدَنا داوُدَ ذَا الْأَيْدِ إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ‌).

"Ayd" (أيد) has been interpreted as both "power" and "blessing."

Dawud was "Dha al-Ayd" (possessor of strength/blessings) in both senses. His physical strength was such that in the battlefield of the Children of Israel against the tyrannical giant Jalut (Goliath), he struck Jalut with a single powerful blow from a stone released from a sling, knocking him from his mount to the dust, where he wallowed in his own blood.

Some have written that the stone split his chest and came out the other side! In terms of political power, he possessed a mighty government that stood with full strength against enemies; it is even said that thousands of people stood guard around his prayer niche (mihrab) from night until morning in a state of readiness.

In terms of spiritual and moral power and the strength of worship, he was such that he remained awake for much of the night engaged in the worship of his Lord and fasted for half the days of the year.

Regarding blessings, Allah had bestowed upon him all kinds of outward and inward favors. In short, Dawud was a man powerful in war, in worship, in knowledge, and in governance, as well as a possessor of abundant blessings. [^1] "Awwab" is derived from the root "awb" (أوب), meaning a voluntary return to something. Since "Awwab" is an intensive form (sighah al-mubalaghah), it indicates that he turned back to his Lord very frequently, repenting for even the slightest oversight or "leaving the better way" (tark al-awla).

Then, following the method of brevity followed by detail commonly used in the Holy Quran when mentioning various matters, after the general mention of Allah's blessings upon Dawud, it proceeds to detail a portion of them, saying: "Indeed, We subjected the mountains [to praise] with him, exalting [Allah] in the evening and at sunrise" (إِنَّا سَخَّرْنَا الْجِبالَ مَعَهُ يُسَبِّحْنَ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَ الْإِشْراقِ‌). [^2]

Not only the mountains, but "the birds as well, gathered together, all were turning to Him [or him]" (وَ الطَّيْرَ مَحْشُورَةً كُلٌّ لَهُ أَوَّابٌ‌). All these birds and mountains were obedient to Dawud's command, echoing his voice and "returning to him."

The pronoun "lahu" (for him/Him) might refer to "Dawud," in which case the meaning would be as stated above. It is also possible that it refers to the Pure Essence of Allah, meaning that all particles of the universe return to Him and are submissive to His command.

There is discussion among commentators as to how the mountains and birds echoed Dawud:

  1. Some have suggested it was Dawud's captivating, attractive, and resonant voice that reflected off the mountains and attracted the birds (though this would not be considered a significant virtue for the Quran to mention with such grandeur).

  2. Others have said that this glorification (tasbih) was accompanied by an audible sound and a form of perception and consciousness inherent within the particles of the universe. According to this view, all beings in the world possess a type of intellect and awareness, and when they heard the enchanting voice of this great Prophet during his supplications, they joined him in harmony, and the clamor of their glorification blended together.

  3. Some have also suggested that this refers to the "existential glorification" (tasbih takwini) which all beings possess through their very state of existence, as the system of their creation clearly testifies that Allah is pure and free from any defect and possesses knowledge, power, and all attributes of perfection.

However, this meaning is not exclusive to Dawud and would not be counted among his unique characteristics. Therefore, the second interpretation is the most appropriate, and this is not beyond the power of God. This was a murmur that always flowed within these beings and in their hidden depths, but Allah, through the power of miracle, made it manifest for Dawud, just as is famously reported regarding the glorification of the pebbles in the palm of the Prophet of Islam ص.

The next verse continues the mention of Allah's blessings upon Dawud, stating:

"And We strengthened his kingdom" (وَ شَدَدْنا مُلْكَهُ‌).

Such that all the rebellious, the tyrants, and the enemies stood in awe of him.

Furthermore, "We gave him wisdom and decisive speech" (وَ آتَيْناهُ الْحِكْمَةَ وَ فَصْلَ الْخِطابِ‌).

The same wisdom about which the Quran says: "And whoever is given wisdom has certainly been given much good" (وَمَنْ يُؤْتَ الْحِكْمَةَ فَقَدْ أُوتِيَ خَيْراً كَثِيراً).

"Wisdom" (hikmah) here means knowledge, the power to manage state affairs, the station of prophethood, or all of these combined.

"Wisdom" sometimes has a theoretical aspect, referred to as "sublime gnosis," and sometimes a practical aspect, referred to as "ethics and righteous deeds," and Dawud had an ample share of all of these.

The final great blessing of Allah upon Dawud was: "We gave him the knowledge of judgment and correct, just arbitration" (وَ فَصْلَ الْخِطابِ‌).

The expression "fasl al-khitab" (decisive speech) for judgment is because "khitab" refers to the arguments of the two disputing parties, and "fasl" means to cut or separate.

We know that the arguments of disputants are cut short only when a correct judgment is made between them; hence, this expression means "just judgment."

There is also the possibility in interpreting this phrase that Allah placed at Dawud's disposal a powerful logic reflecting high thought and depth of insight—not only in the position of judgment but everywhere, he would deliver the final and most decisive word.

Truly, with a God who has the power to grant a worthy human such strength and ability, there is no room for anyone to despair of His grace. This was not only a source of consolation for the Prophet ص and the believers in Mecca who were under severe pressure at that time, but it is a source of comfort for all oppressed believers in all ages and centuries.

Key Points Ten Outstanding Attributes of Dawud (ع)

Some commentators have derived ten great divine favors for Dawud from the above verses, which clarify both the lofty station of this Prophet and the characteristics of a "Perfect Human":

  1. Allah commands the Prophet of Islam ص، despite his own greatness, to follow Dawud in patience and to seek help from his history (اصْبِرْ عَلى‌ ما يَقُولُونَ وَ اذْكُرْ).

  2. He describes him with the station of "servitude" (ubudiyyah), counting this as his first characteristic (عَبْدَنا داوُدَ). A similar description is found regarding the Prophet of Islam ص in the context of the Night Journey (Mi'raj): "Exalted is He who took His servant by night..." (سُبْحانَ الَّذِي أَسْرى‌ بِعَبْدِهِ ...) (Isra: 1).

  3. He possessed strength and power (for obedience to the Lord, avoiding sin, and managing the affairs of the state) (ذَا الْأَيْدِ), just as we read regarding the Prophet of Islam: "It is He who supported you with His help and with the believers" (هُوَ الَّذِي أَيَّدَكَ بِنَصْرِهِ وَ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ) (Anfal: 62).

  4. He describes him as being "Awwab," which means repeatedly returning and constantly turning toward the threshold of Allah's sanctity (إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ‌).

  5. He counts the subjection of the mountains to glorify with him in the mornings and evenings as one of his honors (إِنَّا سَخَّرْنَا الْجِبالَ مَعَهُ يُسَبِّحْنَ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَ الْإِشْراقِ‌).

  6. The harmony of the birds with him in prayer and glorification of God is listed as one of his God-given favors (وَ الطَّيْرَ مَحْشُورَةً).

  7. Not only were they in harmony with him at the start, but every time he returned to the glorification of Allah, they echoed him (كُلٌّ لَهُ أَوَّابٌ‌).

  8. Allah gave him a kingdom and government whose foundations He had made firm, providing him with the material and spiritual means to achieve this goal (وَ شَدَدْنا مُلْكَهُ‌).

  9. Another important God-given asset was extraordinary knowledge—the kind of knowledge that, wherever it exists, is a source of "much good" and the fountainhead of every blessing (وَ آتَيْناهُ الْحِكْمَةَ).

  10. Finally, he was granted powerful logic, effective and influential speech, and the ability to make decisive and just judgments (وَ فَصْلَ الْخِطابِ‌). [^1] Indeed, the foundations of no government can be firm without these attributes: knowledge, power of logic, God-wariness (taqwa), the ability to control the self, and attaining the station of servitude to the Lord.

[^1]: "Ayd" is the plural of "yad" (hand). Subsequently, because the hand is a symbol of power, bestowing blessings, authority, and ownership, it has been used in all these meanings.

[^2]: "Ma'ahu" (with him) may be related to "yusabbihna" (they glorify), thus describing the mountains echoing Dawud, similar to what appears in Surah Saba, verse 10: "O mountains, repeat [Our] praises with him." Or it may be related to "sakhkharna" (We subjected), in which case the meaning is "We subjected the mountains to him," but the use of "ma'ahu" instead of "lahu" (for him) is to highlight that this subjection was in the context of harmony in glorification.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kabir, Fakhr al-Razi, under the verses discussed, Vol. 26, p. 183.

Ayahs

۞ وَهَلْ أَتَىٰكَ نَبَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَصْمِ إِذْ تَسَوَّرُوا۟ ٱلْمِحْرَابَ (٢١)
Has there not come to you the account of the contenders, when they scaled the wall into the sanctuary? (21)
إِذْ دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ دَاوُۥدَ فَفَزِعَ مِنْهُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ لَا تَخَفْ ۖ خَصْمَانِ بَغَىٰ بَعْضُنَا عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلَا تُشْطِطْ وَٱهْدِنَآ إِلَىٰ سَوَآءِ ٱلصِّرَٰطِ (٢٢)
When they entered into the presence of David, he was alarmed by them. They said, ‘Do not be afraid. [We are only] two contenders: one of us has bullied the other. So judge justly between us, and do not exceed [the bounds of justice], and show us the right path.’ (22)
إِنَّ هَـٰذَآ أَخِى لَهُۥ تِسْعٌ وَتِسْعُونَ نَعْجَةً وَلِىَ نَعْجَةٌ وَٰحِدَةٌ فَقَالَ أَكْفِلْنِيهَا وَعَزَّنِى فِى ٱلْخِطَابِ (٢٣)
‘This brother of mine has ninety-nine ewes, while I have only a single ewe, and [yet] he says, ‘Commit it to my care,’ and he browbeats me in speech.’ (23)
قَالَ لَقَدْ ظَلَمَكَ بِسُؤَالِ نَعْجَتِكَ إِلَىٰ نِعَاجِهِۦ ۖ وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلْخُلَطَآءِ لَيَبْغِى بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَقَلِيلٌ مَّا هُمْ ۗ وَظَنَّ دَاوُۥدُ أَنَّمَا فَتَنَّـٰهُ فَٱسْتَغْفَرَ رَبَّهُۥ وَخَرَّ رَاكِعًا وَأَنَابَ ۩ (٢٤)
He said, ‘He has certainly wronged you by asking your ewe in addition to his own ewes, and indeed many partners bully one another, except such as have faith and do righteous deeds, and few are they.’ Then David knew that We had tested him, whereat he pleaded with his Lord for forgiveness, and fell down in prostration and repented. (24)
فَغَفَرْنَا لَهُۥ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ وَإِنَّ لَهُۥ عِندَنَا لَزُلْفَىٰ وَحُسْنَ مَـَٔابٍ (٢٥)
So We forgave him that, and indeed he has [a station of] nearness with Us and a good destination. (25)

Commentary

David's Great Ordeal!

In these verses, a simple and clear discussion regarding the judgment of David (عليه السلام) is presented, which, due to distortions and misinterpretations by some uninformed individuals, has stirred a great controversy among commentators. The waves of this outcry were so strong that they even swept away some Islamic commentators, leading to incorrect and sometimes very offensive judgments regarding this great Prophet.

We will first present the text of the Quranic verses here without any explanation so that readers may grasp the meaning of the verses with an open mind. After this brief commentary, we will address the various discussions that have taken place in this regard.

Following the previous verses that described the unique qualities of David (عليه السلام) and God's great blessings upon him, the Quran explains an incident that occurred during a judicial proceeding for David:

First, addressing the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), It says: "Has the story of the litigants reached you, when they climbed over the wall of the sanctuary (Mihrab) of David?" (وَ هَلْ أَتاكَ نَبَأُ الْخَصْمِ إِذْ تَسَوَّرُوا الْمِحْرابَ).

The word "Khasm" (خصم) originally has an infinitive meaning, signifying "to dispute," but it is frequently used to refer to the parties involved in a dispute as well. This word is applied to both singular and plural, and sometimes its plural form appears as "Khusum" (خصوم).

"Tasawwaru" (تسوروا) is derived from the root "Sur" (سور), meaning a high wall that surrounds a house or a city. However, it should be noted that this root originally means "to jump" or "to climb."

"Mihrab" (محراب) refers to the head of a gathering or upper chambers; and because the place of worship was located there, it gradually came to be used to mean "temple" or "sanctuary." In everyday usage, it specifically refers to the place where the Imam stands to lead the congregational prayer.

In Al-Mufradat, it is narrated from some that the Mihrab of a mosque is called such because it is the place of Harb (war) and battle against Satan and the whims of the self.

In any case, despite David (عليه السلام) having many guards and watchers around him, the disputing parties climbed over the wall of his sanctuary and palace through an unconventional route and suddenly appeared before him, as the Quran continues: "When they entered upon David [without permission or prior notice], and he was terrified of them" because he thought they had evil intentions toward him (إِذْ دَخَلُوا عَلى‌ داوُدَ فَفَزِعَ مِنْهُمْ‌).

But they quickly dispelled his fear and "said: 'Fear not! We are two litigants, one of whom has wronged the other'" and we have come to you for judgment (قالُوا لا تَخَفْ خَصْمانِ بَغى‌ بَعْضُنا عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ‌).

"So judge between us with truth, and do not act unjustly, and guide us to the even path" (فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَنا بِالْحَقِّ وَ لا تُشْطِطْ وَ اهْدِنا إِلى‌ سَواءِ الصِّراطِ). "Tushtit" (تشطط) is from the root "Shatat" (شطط), which originally means "great distance." Since injustice and oppression distance a person greatly from the truth, the word Shatat is used in this sense, and it is also applied to speech that is far from reality.

Certainly, David's anxiety and fear subsided at this point, but perhaps this question still remained for him: "Very well, you have no evil intent and your goal is to complain to a judge, but for what purpose did you come through this unconventional way?"

However, they did not give David much time, and one stepped forward to present the complaint, saying: "Indeed, this is my brother; he has ninety-nine ewes, and I have but one ewe. Yet he says, 'Entrust it to me,' and he has overpowered me in speech" (إِنَّ هذا أَخِي لَهُ تِسْعٌ وَ تِسْعُونَ نَعْجَةً وَ لِيَ نَعْجَةٌ واحِدَةٌ فَقالَ أَكْفِلْنِيها وَ عَزَّنِي فِي الْخِطابِ‌).

"Na'jah" (نعجة) means a ewe (female sheep), and it is also applied to wild cows and female mountain sheep.

"Akfilniha" (أكفلنيها) is from the root "Kafalat" (guardianship/surety), and here it is an allusion to "handing over" (the meaning of the sentence is: "Entrust its guardianship to me," i.e., "Give it to me").

"Azzani" (عزني) is from the root "Izzat" (might/power), meaning "to overcome," and the concept of the sentence is that "he overpowered me."

At this point, before hearing the words of the opposing party—as the appearance of the Quranic verses suggests—David turned to the complainant and "said: 'He has certainly wronged you by demanding your ewe to be added to his ewes!'" (قالَ لَقَدْ ظَلَمَكَ بِسُؤالِ نَعْجَتِكَ إِلى‌ نِعاجِهِ‌).

But this is nothing new: "And indeed, many associates and people who deal with one another oppress one another" (وَ إِنَّ كَثِيراً مِنَ الْخُلَطاءِ لَيَبْغِي بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ‌) [1] "except those who believe and do righteous deeds" (إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ‌).

"And how few they are!" (وَ قَلِيلٌ ما هُمْ‌) [2]. Yes, those who fully observe the rights of others in social interaction and friendship and do not allow the slightest transgression against their friends are few. Only those who possess a sufficient capital of faith and righteous deeds can fulfill the rights of friends and acquaintances in a completely just manner.

In any case, it seems that the disputing parties were satisfied upon hearing this speech...

[1] "Khulata" (خلطاء) is the plural of "Khalit" (خليط), meaning persons or things that are mixed together; it is also applied to friends, partners, and neighbors. Although oppression is not exclusive to them, this group is specifically mentioned either because of the contact they have with each other—where friction inevitably occurs—or because oppression is not expected from them.

[2] The structure of the sentence is such that "hum" is the subject (mubtada) and "qalil" is the predicate (khabar), and "ma" is extra (za'idah), used here for emphasis on the scarcity and fewness.

...and they left David's assembly.

But David then fell into deep thought. Although he knew he had rendered a just judgment—for if the opposing party did not accept the complainant's claim, he would surely have protested, and his silence was the best proof that the matter was as the complainant stated—nevertheless, the etiquette of a judicial assembly required that David should not have hurried in his speech. Rather, he should have personally questioned the opposing party and then judged. Therefore, he deeply regretted this action of his "and David realized that We had only tried him" (وَ ظَنَّ داوُدُ أَنَّما فَتَنَّاهُ‌).

He turned to seek forgiveness "and he asked forgiveness of his Lord and fell down bowing and turned [to Him] in repentance" (فَاسْتَغْفَرَ رَبَّهُ وَ خَرَّ راكِعاً وَ أَنابَ‌).

"Kharra" (خر) is from the root "Kharir", meaning to fall from a height accompanied by a sound, like the sound of a waterfall. Since those who prostrate seem to fall from a height and recite glorifications (tasbih) during prostration, this expression is used as an allusion to prostrating.

The expression "raki'an" (bowing) in the verse under discussion is either because Ruku' also means prostration in the language, or because Ruku' is a prelude to prostration.

In any case, Allah included him in His grace and forgave his slip in this "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla), as the Quran says in the next verse: "So We forgave him that" (فَغَفَرْنا لَهُ ذلِكَ‌).

"And indeed, for him with Us is a near access and a good place of return" (وَ إِنَّ لَهُ عِنْدَنا لَزُلْفى‌ وَ حُسْنَ مَآبٍ‌).

"Zulfa" (زلفى) means status (and proximity in the presence of God), and "Husna Ma'ab" refers to Paradise and the blessings of the Hereafter.

Key Points

1- What was the actual story of David?

What can be derived from the Holy Quran is no more than this: individuals climbed over David's sanctuary to seek justice and appeared before him. He was initially terrified, then listened to the complainant's grievance that one of the two had 99 ewes and the other had only one, while the owner of the ninety-nine requested his brother to entrust the single one to him as well. He (David) gave the right to the complainant and called this request oppression and transgression. Then he regretted his action, asked for God's forgiveness, and God forgave him.

However, two expressions here require careful consideration: one is the issue of the "test" (azmayesh) and the other is the issue of "seeking forgiveness and repentance" (istighfar and tawbah).

The Quran has not pointed to a specific detail in these two parts. However, considering the existing clues in these verses and the Islamic narrations that have come in the interpretation of these verses, David had great knowledge and skill in the matter of judgment, and God wanted to test him. Therefore, He brought about such unusual circumstances (entering upon David through an unconventional way from above the sanctuary) for him. He was caught in haste and hurry, and before asking for an explanation from the opposing party, he rendered a judgment, even though the judgment was just.

Although he soon realized his slip and compensated before time passed, nevertheless, whatever it was, an act proceeded from him that was not befitting the high station of prophethood. Therefore, he sought forgiveness for this "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla), and Allah included him in His pardon and forgiveness.

The proof for this interpretation—in addition to what has passed—is the verse that comes immediately after these verses, addressing David: "We have made you a successor (Khalifa) on the earth, so judge between people with truth and justice and do not follow desire."

This expression shows that David's slip was in the manner of judgment and adjudication.

In this way, there is nothing in the above verses that contradicts the status and station of this great Prophet.

2- The Mythical Story of the Torah Regarding David

Now we turn to the Torah to see what it says in this regard, and also to find the root of some interpretations by uninformed and ignorant individuals.

The Torah, in the Second Book of Samuel, Chapter 11, verses 2 to 27, says:

"And it came to pass in an eveningtide, that David arose from off his bed, and walked upon the roof of the king's house: and from the roof he saw a woman washing herself; and the woman was very beautiful to look upon. And David sent and enquired after the woman. And one said, Is not this Bathsheba [1], the daughter of Eliam, the wife of Uriah the Hittite [2]?

And David sent messengers, and took her; and she came in unto him, and he lay with her; for she was purified from her uncleanness: and she returned unto her house. And the woman conceived, and sent and told David, and said, I am with child. And David sent to Joab [3], saying, Send me Uriah the Hittite. And Joab sent Uriah to David. And when Uriah was come unto him, David demanded of him how Joab did, and how the people did, and how the war prospered.

And David said to Uriah, Go down to thy house, and wash thy feet. And Uriah departed out of the king's house, and there followed him a mess of meat from the king. But Uriah slept at the door of the king's house with all the servants of his lord, and went not down to his house. And when they had told David, saying, Uriah went not down unto his house, David said unto Uriah, Camest thou not from thy journey? why then didst thou not go down unto thine house? And Uriah said unto David, The ark, and Israel, and Judah, abide in tents; and my lord Joab, and the servants of my lord, are encamped in the open fields; shall I then go into mine house, to eat and to drink, and to lie with my wife? as thou livest, and as thy soul liveth, I will not do this thing...

And it came to pass in the morning, that David wrote a letter to Joab, and sent it by the hand of Uriah. And he wrote in the letter, saying, Set ye Uriah in the forefront of the hottest battle, and retire ye from him, that he may be smitten, and die.

And it came to pass, when Joab observed the city, that he assigned Uriah unto a place where he knew that valiant men were. And the men of the city went out, and fought with Joab: and there fell some of the people of the servants of David; and Uriah the Hittite died also... And when the wife of Uriah heard that Uriah her husband was dead, she mourned for her husband. And when the mourning was past, David sent and fetched her to his house, and she became his wife!... But the thing that David had done displeased the Lord!" [4]

In summary, this story up to this point is as follows:

One day David goes to the roof of the palace and his eyes fall upon the neighboring house; he sees a woman naked while washing herself. Love for her takes place in his heart. By whatever means, he brings her to his house, and she becomes pregnant by David! The husband of this woman was one of the prominent officers of David's army, a man of pure nature and sincerity. David (we seek refuge in Allah) kills him through a cowardly conspiracy by sending him to a dangerous zone in the war, and officially marries his wife!! Now hear the rest of the story from the tongue of the current Torah:

In Chapter 12 of the same Second Book of Samuel, it is stated: "The Lord sent Nathan (one of the prophets of the Children of Israel and David's advisor) to David. He said: There were two men in a city, one rich and the other poor. The rich man had many sheep and cattle, and the poor man had nothing but one little ewe lamb. A traveler came to the rich man; he was unwilling to take from his own sheep to prepare food for the guest. He took the poor man's lamb and killed it. Now what should be done?! David was very angry and said to Nathan: As the Lord lives, the man who has done this deserves to die! He must pay four sheep for the lamb! But Nathan said to David: You are that man! David realized his wrong deed and repented. God accepted his repentance, yet He brought heavy calamities upon David."

Here, the Torah has expressions that the pen is ashamed to mention, so we omit them.

In this part of the Torah's story, some points are particularly noteworthy:

1- No one came to David seeking justice; rather, one of the prophets, his advisor, mentioned a story as an example for the purpose of admonition. There is no talk of two brothers and the request of one from the other here; rather, it is about two men, rich and poor, one having many cattle and sheep and the other only one lamb, but the rich man killed the poor man's lamb for his guest. Up to here, there is no mention of climbing the wall of the sanctuary, nor David's fear, nor the filing of a lawsuit between two brothers, nor a request for forgiveness.

2- David considered that oppressive rich man deserving of death (Why death for one sheep?).

3- Immediately, he issued a ruling contrary to this ruling and said he must give four sheep instead of one sheep? (Why?).

4- David confessed to his sin regarding the betrayal of Uriah's wife.

5- God forgave him (Why so simply?).

6- God imposed a strange punishment on David, which is better left unmentioned.

7- And this same woman—with such a "brilliant" background—became the mother of Solomon! Although recounting these stories is truly painful, what can be done? Some uninformed ignorant people, under the influence of these Israelite narrations, have darkened the pure face of the verses of the Holy Quran and said things that left no choice but to mention part of this scandalous story to clarify the truth.

[1] "Bathsheba" is the name of the woman whom David—according to the Torah—saw naked from the roof, and the fire of love for her was ignited in his heart. This woman was the daughter of Eliam, one of the Hebrew officials.

[2] "Uriah" was the name of one of the senior officers of David's army, and "Hittite" refers to the descendants of Heth, son of Canaan.

[3] "Joab" was the commander of David's army.

[4] Quoted from the Second Book of Samuel, Chapter 11, verses 2 to 27.

Now we ask

1- Is it possible for a Prophet whom God has praised in the previous verses with ten great descriptions and directed the Prophet of Islam to take inspiration from his life story, to be subject to even one-thousandth of these accusations?!

2- Are these absurdities compatible with the sentence that the Quran says in the verses following this: "O David, indeed We have made you a successor (Khalifa) upon the earth"?

3- Not a Prophet of God—if an ordinary person committed such a crime, snatching the wife of his loyal, pure, and faithful officer from him in such a cowardly manner, what would people judge about him and what would be his punishment?! Even if this act proceeded from the most wicked of the wicked, it would be surprising.

It is true that the Torah does not consider David a Prophet, but it introduces him as a just king who held a very esteemed position and was the founder of the great temple of the Children of Israel.

4- Interestingly, one of the famous books of the Torah is the "Psalms of David" and his supplications. Can the supplications and words of such a person be placed within the Heavenly Books?

5- Anyone with the slightest intellect and understanding knows that the stories of the current distorted Torah in this regard are myths crafted by the enemies of the school of the Prophets or by very uninformed and ignorant individuals. How can they be made the criterion for discussion?

Yes, the greatness of the Quran lies in the fact that it is free from such myths.

3- Islamic Narrations and the Incident of David (عليه السلام)

In Islamic narrations, the ugly and mythical story of the Torah has been denied in the strongest possible terms. Among them, in a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), it is narrated that he said:

"Whoever is brought to me claiming that David married the wife of Uriah, I will surely administer two legal punishments (hadd) upon him: one for [slandering] prophethood and one for Islam." [1]

This is because the above attribution is, on one hand, attributing an illegitimate act to a believing person, and on the other hand, it is a violation of the status of prophethood; therefore, the punishment for slander (qadhf) (80 lashes each time) must be executed twice in his case. This same meaning has been narrated from that great Imam in another expression:

"Whoever relates to you the Hadith of David according to what the storytellers say, I will give him one hundred and sixty lashes." [2]

In another Hadith that Saduq has narrated in Al-Amali from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read:

"Indeed, the satisfaction of people cannot be possessed, and their tongues cannot be controlled. Did they not attribute to David that he followed a bird until he looked at the wife of Uriah and fell in love with her, and that he sent her husband in front of the Ark until he was killed, then he married her?!" (Where the great Prophet of God is not safe from the tongues of the people, what can others expect?) [3]

Finally, in a Hadith in Uyun al-Akhbar from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام), it is mentioned that during a discussion with the leaders of various religions regarding the infallibility of the Prophets, he said to one of those present (Ali ibn al-Jahm): "What do you say about David?" He said: "They say David was busy with worship in his sanctuary when Satan appeared before him in the form of a beautiful bird. David broke his prayer and followed the bird!..."

Then he explained the myth of seeing Uriah's wife while she was bathing, falling in love with her, sending her husband in front of the Ark to the battlefield, his being killed, and David's marriage to his wife.

Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام) became very upset, struck his blessed forehead, and said:

"إِنَّا لِلَّهِ وَ إِنَّا إِلَيْهِ راجِعُونَ (Indeed, we belong to Allah and to Him we shall return). You have attributed to a Prophet from the Prophets of Allah negligence in his prayer until he went out following a bird, then [you attributed to him] indecency, and then the murder of an innocent human being?!"

Ali ibn al-Jahm asked: "Then what was David's sin for which he sought forgiveness and which is mentioned in the Quran?"

The Imam (عليه السلام) in response explained David's haste in the matter of judgment and used the next verse: "O David, indeed We have made you a successor (Khalifa) upon the earth" as evidence.

Ali ibn al-Jahm asked: "Then what was the story of Uriah?"

The Imam said: "In the time of David, women whose husbands passed away or were killed never married again (and this was a source of many corruptions). The first person for whom Allah made this permissible was David (so that this tradition would be broken, and women who had lost their husbands would be relieved from their state of limbo). Therefore, David, after Uriah was killed (by chance in one of the wars), married his wife, and this was difficult for the people of that time to accept (and following that, myths were woven together)." [4]

From this Hadith, it is understood that the issue of "Uriah" had a simple real root, which David performed as a divine mission. But wise enemies on one hand, and ignorant friends on the other, and storytellers who are accustomed to presenting strange and false matters on the third hand, created branches and leaves for this story that make a person shudder.

One said: "Surely this marriage did not take place without a prelude?" Another said: "Surely Uriah's house was in David's neighborhood!" And finally, to make David's eyes fall upon Uriah's wife, they wove the myth of the bird, and ultimately, in total, they accused a great Prophet of various shameful major sins, and foolish uninformed people also passed it from tongue to tongue, such that if it had not been mentioned in famous books, we would have considered even its mention wrong.

Of course, this narration does not contradict what came in the narration of the Commander of the Faithful Ali (عليه السلام), because his words refer to the famous false story that attributes adultery and the like (we seek refuge in Allah) to this great Prophet.

[1] Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed. [2] Tafsir Fakhr Razi, under the verses discussed. [3] Amali al-Saduq, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 446. [4] Uyun al-Akhbar, as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 445.

Justifications of the Commentators

Some commentators have offered other justifications for the story of David. Although they are not consistent with the appearance of the verses, we do not find it inappropriate to mention some of them to complete the discussion.
