1- Among them is that Prophet David had divided his hours with an organized schedule, and he did not receive petitioners except at specific hours. One day, two people who intended to kill him wanted to come to him while David was busy worshipping the Lord in the sanctuary. They took advantage of the opportunity and climbed over his sanctuary. When they came to him, they saw guards around and were afraid, so they immediately fabricated a lie, saying: "We are two litigants who have come to you for justice," and they explained the incident that the Quran mentions. David judged between them, but since he was aware that this staging was for the purpose of killing him, he became angry and decided to take revenge on them. But it was not long before he regretted this decision and sought forgiveness. [1]

2- The great commentator, the author of Al-Mizan, has an explanation here that is fundamentally in harmony with what other great Islamic commentators have said in the interpretation of this incident of David, which we also mentioned above, but it differs in some aspects, which follows:

"Many commentators believe that the two litigants who entered upon David were from the angels of God whom God sent to test David.

However, the specifics of the story, such as their climbing over the sanctuary and entering upon David in an unusual manner, and his fear and terror, as well as the realization that this incident was a divine test—all these show that this incident was in the form of "representation" (tamathul) of angels in the appearance of men of the human type.

(The meaning of tamathul is that such individuals did not actually come to David in external existence; rather, it was reflected as such in David's power of perception).

Therefore, the ruling he issued in this dispute was a ruling in the realm of tamathul, just as if he had seen them in a dream. Just as a person has no religious obligation (taklif) in the events of the world of dreams, there is no taklif in the realm of tamathul either. Taklif is in the manifest world, i.e., the material world. And if a mistake proceeded from him, it was in this very realm of tamathul, and it is not something that is incompatible with the status of infallibility (ismah), similar to the mistake of Adam in Paradise before he descended to the earth, which is the place of taklif and legislation. In this way, his seeking of forgiveness is not seeking forgiveness for an actual sin." [2]

But certainly, the appearance of the verses is that this complaint and filing of the lawsuit were from individuals who had external reality. Nevertheless, the said judgment was not a sin that proceeded from David after he had obtained knowledge and certainty from the complainant's words, even though the recommended etiquette of judgment required that he should not hurry in judgment, and his seeking of forgiveness was also from this "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla).

In any case, it is not necessary to consider the incident of this adjudication to be in the realm of tamathul, or as others have said, to consider it a staging for David's alertness and awakening. It is better to preserve the appearance of the verses and interpret them in the order mentioned, which both preserves the literal appearances of the verse and avoids any problem regarding the status of the infallibility of the Prophets.

[1] In Tafsir Fakhr Razi and Ruh al-Ma'ani, this discussion is mentioned as a justification, and Maraghi has accepted it in his commentary. [2] Al-Mizan, Vol. 17, p. 203.

Ayahs

يَـٰدَاوُۥدُ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَـٰكَ خَلِيفَةً فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلَا تَتَّبِعِ ٱلْهَوَىٰ فَيُضِلَّكَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌۢ بِمَا نَسُوا۟ يَوْمَ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٢٦)
‘O David! Indeed, We have made you a vicegerent on the earth. So judge between people with justice, and do not follow your desires, or they will lead you astray from the way of Allah. Indeed there is a severe punishment for those who stray from the way of Allah, because of their forgetting the Day of Reckoning.’ (26)
وَمَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَآءَ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا بَـٰطِلًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ظَنُّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلنَّارِ (٢٧)
We did not create the sky and the earth and whatever is between them in vain. That is a conjecture of the faithless. So woe to the faithless for the Fire! (27)
أَمْ نَجْعَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ كَٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ كَٱلْفُجَّارِ (٢٨)
Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the Godwary like the vicious? (28)
كِتَـٰبٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ إِلَيْكَ مُبَـٰرَكٌ لِّيَدَّبَّرُوٓا۟ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ وَلِيَتَذَكَّرَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (٢٩)
[This is] a blessed Book that We have sent down to you, so that they may contemplate its signs, and that those who possess intellect may take admonition. (29)

Commentary

Judge with Justice and Do Not Follow Lowly Desires!

Following the story of Dawud (peace be upon him), and as a concluding word, Allah addresses him, describing his lofty station while outlining his heavy duties and responsibilities in a decisive tone and meaningful expressions, saying: "O Dawud! We have indeed made you a vicegerent (and Our representative) on earth; so judge between people with truth, and do not follow lowly desire, for it will mislead you from the path of Allah. Indeed, those who go astray from the path of Allah will have a severe punishment because they forgot the Day of Account" (يَا دَاوُودُ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَاكَ خَلِيفَةً فِي الْأَرْضِ فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِالْحَقِّ وَلَا تَتَّبِعِ الْهَوَىٰ فَيُضِلَّكَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِمَا نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسَابِ).

The content of this verse, which speaks of Dawud's high station and his important duty, demonstrates how baseless are the false legends woven regarding his marriage to the wife of "Uriah."

How is it possible for Allah to grant vicegerency on earth and entrust the station of absolute judgment to someone who has cast a treacherous eye upon the honor of the believers and his own companions, and whose hands are stained with the blood of the innocent?! This verse consists of five sentences, each pursuing a specific truth:

First is the station of Dawud's vicegerency (Khilafah) on earth. Does this refer to succeeding previous prophets or to Divine vicegerency? The second meaning seems more appropriate and is more consistent with verse 30 of Surah Al-Baqarah: "وَإِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً" ("And remember when your Lord said to the angels: 'Indeed, I am making a vicegerent on the earth'").

Of course, vicegerency in the literal sense of the word does not apply to Allah, as it is only valid for those who pass away or are absent; rather, it refers to his representation among His servants and the execution of His orders and commands on earth.

This sentence indicates that governance on earth must originate from Divine governance, and any government established through other means is an oppressive and usurping one.

In the second sentence, He commands: Now that this great blessing has been bestowed upon you, your duty is to judge between people with truth. In reality, the result of Divine vicegerency is the governance of truth, and from this sentence, it can be inferred that the governance of truth also arises only from Divine vicegerency and is its direct product.

In the third sentence, He points to the most significant danger threatening a just ruler, saying: "Never follow lowly desire."

Indeed, lowly desire casts a thick veil over the truth-seeking eyes of man and creates a separation between him and justice.

Therefore, in the fourth sentence, He says: "If you follow lowly desire, it will prevent you from the path of Allah, which is the path of truth."

Thus, wherever there is misguidance, lowly desire is involved, and wherever there is lowly desire, the result is misguidance.

A ruler who follows lowly desire sacrifices the interests and rights of the people for his own greed, and for this reason, his government will be unstable and faced with failure.

It is possible that "lowly desire" (Hawa) here has a broad meaning that includes both the person's own desires and the whims of the people. In this way, the Quran strikes a line of cancellation through schools of thought that consider following public opinion—whatever it may be—as necessary for governments, because the result of both is straying from the path of Allah and the road of truth.

Today, we witness the disastrous effects of this way of thinking in the so-called civilized world, where sometimes the most heinous and ugly acts are given legal form due to the inclinations of the people, reaching a level of scandal that the pen is ashamed to describe.

It is true that the foundations of government must rest on the shoulders of the people and be realized through public participation, but this does not mean that the criterion for right and wrong in everything and everywhere should be the inclinations of the majority.

Government must have a framework of truth but seek help from the power of society in implementing this framework. This is the meaning of the "Islamic Republic" that we seek, which is composed of the two words "Republic" and "Islamic"; in other words, the principles are taken from the school of thought (Islam), and the execution is carried out with the participation of the people (take note).

Finally, in the fifth sentence, He points to the fact that straying from the path of truth stems from forgetting the "Day of Account," and its result is severe Divine punishment.

Fundamentally, forgetting the Day of Resurrection is always the source of misguidance, and every misguidance is mixed with this forgetfulness. This principle clarifies the educational impact of mindfulness regarding the Hereafter in human life.

The narrations mentioned in Islamic sources in this regard are very worthy of attention, including the famous Hadith narrated from the noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) and from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), who said:

"O people! The most terrifying things I fear for you are two: following lowly desires and long-reaching hopes. As for following lowly desires, it prevents one from the truth; and as for long-reaching hopes, they cause the Hereafter to be forgotten." [1] It is fitting that this sentence be written in gold and placed before the eyes of everyone, especially rulers, judges, and officials.

In another narration from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), we read:

"Three things are destructive: stinginess that is obeyed, lowly desire that is followed, and a person being pleased with himself!" [2]

Then, following the discussion of Dawud's story and his Divine vicegerency on earth, He speaks of the purposeful nature of the universe so that the direction of governance on earth—which is a part of it—becomes clear. He says: "We have not created the heaven and the earth and whatever is between them in vain. That is the assumption of those who disbelieve; so woe to those who disbelieve from the Fire!" (وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا بَاطِلًا ذَٰلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنَ النَّارِ).

The important issue from which all rights originate is the purposefulness of creation. When we accept in our worldview that this vast universe was not created in vain by the Great God, we immediately seek its purpose—a purpose summarized in the short yet content-rich words "perfection," "education," and "training." From this, we conclude that governments must also move along this line, strengthening the foundations of education and training and becoming the means for the spiritual perfection of humans.

In other words, the universe is established upon truth and justice, and governments must also be in harmony with the entirety of the universe—that is, aligned with the standards of truth and justice.

Furthermore, the last sentence of the previous verse, which spoke of forgetting the Day of Recompense, is also completely harmonious with the content of the verse under discussion. This is because the purpose of the creation of the world necessitates that there be a Day of Recompense. As we have stated in the discussions on the Hereafter (at the end of Surah Ya-Sin), if there were no Day of Account, the creation of this world would be vain, meaningless, hollow, and incomprehensible.

Interestingly, the end of this verse points to one of the clear lines that separates the school of faith from disbelief: the belief in the absurdity of the world in atheistic schools, examples of which we are plagued with today. They explicitly declare that this world is absurd and aimless. With such a worldview, how can they be implementers of truth and justice in their governments?! Only a government can implement truth and justice that originates from a Divine worldview, which acknowledges a purpose for the world and a calculated system that the government must also follow. If today's atheistic world has reached a dead end in its governance, in its war and peace, and in its economy and culture, the root cause must be sought in this very matter. It is also for this reason that they base their activities on "force and dominance," and for everyone, they only recognize what can be obtained through force and oppression. How terrifying is a world founded and managed upon this way of thinking.

In any case, Allah is All-Wise, and it is impossible for Him to create this great universe without a purpose. This purpose will only be fulfilled if this world is a prelude to a wider and more expansive world—a world that connects to eternity and justifies the legitimacy of this world.

In the next verse, He adds: "Shall We treat those who believe and do righteous deeds like the corrupters on earth?" (أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ) [1] "Or shall We treat the righteous like the wicked?" (أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ).

Neither is aimlessness in creation possible, nor is the equality of the righteous and the wicked. This is because the first group walks in the path of the goals of creation and moves toward the destination, while the second group stands in the opposite direction.

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 42. [2]: Kitab al-Khisal, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 453.

[1]: Some commentators have stated that "Am" (or) here is in the sense of "Bal" (rather) for digression, but it is also possible that "Am" is a conjunction for a deleted interrogative, and the estimation is: "Did We create the heavens and the earth in vain, or shall We treat the righteous like the wicked?"

In fact, the discussion of the Hereafter with all its aspects is explained argumentatively in this verse and the previous one:

On one hand, it says: The wisdom of the Creator necessitates that the creation of the world has a purpose (and this purpose is not achieved without another world, for the few days of worldly life are too worthless to be the goal of this great creation).

On the other hand, His wisdom and justice necessitate that the good and the bad, the just and the oppressive, are not equal. This constitutes the entirety of the Resurrection, reward, punishment, Paradise, and Hell.

Beyond this, when we look at the scene of human society in this world, we see the wicked alongside the believers and the bad alongside the good; in fact, in many cases, we find the evil corrupters in greater luxury and comfort. If there were no other world after this one where justice is implemented, the state of this world would be contrary to both "wisdom" and "justice," and this itself is considered another proof for the issue of the Hereafter.

In other words: To prove the Hereafter, sometimes the argument is made through the "Proof of Wisdom" and sometimes through the "Proof of Justice." The previous verse looks at the first argument, and the following verse looks at the second.

In the last verse under discussion, He points to a matter that actually ensures the fulfillment of the purpose of creation, saying: "This is a blessed Book which We have sent down to you, that they may ponder over its verses, and that those of understanding may take heed" (كِتَابٌ أَنْزَلْنَاهُ إِلَيْكَ مُبَارَكٌ لِيَدَّبَّرُوا آيَاتِهِ وَلِيَتَذَكَّرَ أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ).

Its teachings are eternal, its commands are deep and rooted, and its programs are life-giving and guide man on the path toward the goal of creation.

The "purpose" of the revelation of this great Book was not for people to be content only with recitation and lip service; rather, the goal was for its verses to be a source of thought and reflection, and a means for the awakening of consciences, which in turn creates movement in the path of action.

The expression "Mubarak" (blessed), as we know, refers to something that possesses continuous and lasting good. This expression regarding the Quran refers to the permanence of the human society's benefit from its teachings. Since this word is used in an absolute sense, it includes every kind of goodness and happiness in this world and the Hereafter.

In short, whatever good and blessing you desire is within it, provided that you ponder over it, take inspiration from it, and set yourself in motion.

Key Points

1- Piety and Wickedness in Contrast

In the above verses, "corruption on earth" is placed in contrast to "faith and righteous deeds," and "Fujur" (tearing the veil of religion/wickedness) is placed in contrast to "Taqwa" (piety/righteousness).

Are these two expressions of one reality in two different phrasings, or are they two different points?

It is not unlikely that both are an emphasis on one meaning, as the "righteous" (Muttaqin) are the same as the believers who perform righteous deeds, and the "wicked" (Fujjar) are the same as the "corrupters on earth."

It is also possible that the first sentence refers to both ideological and practical aspects, comparing those with correct beliefs and righteous deeds to those with corrupt beliefs and corrupt deeds, while the second sentence refers only to practical aspects.

Another possible difference is that "Taqwa and Fujur" refer to the perfection or deficiency of the individual, while "righteous deeds and corruption on earth" refer to social aspects.

However, emphasis seems more appropriate.

2- To Whom Do These Verses Refer?

In a narration regarding the interpretation of these verses, we read: "Those who believe and do righteous deeds" (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ):

It refers to the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), and his companions, while: "like the corrupters on earth" (كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ): refers to their opponents. [1] In another Hadith narrated by "Ibn Asakir" from "Ibn Abbas," it is stated that "those who believe" refers to Ali (peace be upon him), Hamza, and Ubaydah, who stood in the field of Badr against Utbah, Walid, and Shaybah from the army of polytheism (and engaged them in man-to-man combat and overcame them), and "like the corrupters on earth" refers to the three aforementioned individuals from the army of disbelief and polytheism who stood against them. [2] It is clear that the meaning of these narrations is not to restrict the concept of the verse to specific individuals, but rather to state the occasion of revelation or the clear and prominent examples of this verse.

[1]: Tafsir "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 4, p. 453 (Hadith 37). [2]: Tafsir "Ruh al-Ma'ani," Vol. 23, p. 171.

Ayahs

وَوَهَبْنَا لِدَاوُۥدَ سُلَيْمَـٰنَ ۚ نِعْمَ ٱلْعَبْدُ ۖ إِنَّهُۥٓ أَوَّابٌ (٣٠)
And to David We gave Solomon—what an excellent servant he was! Indeed, he was a penitent [soul]. (30)
إِذْ عُرِضَ عَلَيْهِ بِٱلْعَشِىِّ ٱلصَّـٰفِنَـٰتُ ٱلْجِيَادُ (٣١)
One evening when there were displayed before him prancing steeds, (31)
فَقَالَ إِنِّىٓ أَحْبَبْتُ حُبَّ ٱلْخَيْرِ عَن ذِكْرِ رَبِّى حَتَّىٰ تَوَارَتْ بِٱلْحِجَابِ (٣٢)
he said, ‘Indeed I have preferred the love of [worldly] niceties to the remembrance of my Lord until [the sun] disappeared behind the [night’s] veil.’ (32)
رُدُّوهَا عَلَىَّ ۖ فَطَفِقَ مَسْحًۢا بِٱلسُّوقِ وَٱلْأَعْنَاقِ (٣٣)
‘Bring it back for me!’ Then he [and others] began to wipe [their] legs and necks. (33)

Commentary

Solomon Reviews His Combat Forces

These verses continue the previous discussion regarding David (peace be upon him).

In the first verse, it gives news of granting him a distinguished son like "Solomon," who was the continuer of his government and mission, saying: "And We gifted Solomon to David. What an excellent servant! Indeed, he was ever turning [to Allah]"

(وَ وَهَبْنا لِداوُدَ سُلَيْمانَ نِعْمَ الْعَبْدُ إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ).

This expression, which indicates the greatness of Solomon's status, is perhaps intended to refute the baseless and ugly accusations regarding Solomon's birth from the wife of "Uriah" found in the distorted Torah, which were prevalent in that environment during the era of the Quran's revelation.

The expression "We gifted" (وَهَبْنا) on one hand, the expression "What an excellent servant" (نِعْمَ الْعَبْدُ) on the other, and the reasoning "Indeed, he was ever turning" (إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ)—meaning one who constantly returns to obedience and compliance with Allah's command and repents for the slightest negligence or lapses—on the third hand, all indicate the greatness of the status of this great Prophet.

The expression "Indeed, he was ever turning" (إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ) is exactly the same expression used regarding his father David in verse 17 of this same Surah. Given that "Awwab" is an intensive form (sighah al-mubalaghah) meaning "one who returns frequently," and it is mentioned without restriction or condition, it can signify returning to the obedience of Allah's command, returning to truth and justice, and returning from negligence or "leaving the better option" (tark al-awla).

From the next verse, the story of Solomon's horses begins. Various interpretations have been offered for it, some of which—coming from the uninformed—are quite offensive and contrary to the standards of reason, and even beneath the dignity of an ordinary person, let alone a great Prophet like Solomon (peace be upon him). However, researchers, inspired by rational and traditional evidence, have blocked the path to such interpretations.

Before we explore the various possibilities, we will interpret the verses according to their apparent meaning—or the most apparent possibility—so it becomes clear that these improper attributions are not in the Quran, but rather have been imposed upon the Quran through the preconceptions of others.

The Quran says: "Remember when there were displayed before him (Solomon) in the afternoon well-bred, swift horses" (إِذْ عُرِضَ عَلَيْهِ بِالْعَشِيِّ الصَّافِناتُ الْجِيادُ).

"Safinat" (الصَّافِناتُ) is the plural of "Safinah." As many commentators and lexicographers have written, it refers to horses that, when standing, stand on three legs and slightly raise one foreleg, placing only the tip of the hoof on the ground. This posture is specific to agile and swift horses that are ready to move at any moment. [^1] "Jiyad" (الْجِيادُ) is the plural of "Jawad," meaning fast and swift horses here. It is originally derived from the root "Jud" (generosity); however, "Jud" in humans is through giving wealth, and in horses, it is through speed of travel.

Thus, these horses showed their readiness for movement while stationary and their speed of action while moving.

From the totality of this verse and the various clues surrounding it, it appears that one day in the afternoon, Solomon was reviewing his swift and agile horses which he had prepared for the field of Jihad. The officers paraded the said horses before him. Since a just and influential king must have a powerful army, and one of the important tools of an army is swift mounts, this description is recounted in the Quran after mentioning Solomon's status as an example of his work.

Here, so that it is not imagined that his interest in these powerful horses was worldly, Solomon "said: I have preferred the love of [these] good things because of the remembrance of my Lord" and His command. I want to use them in the field of Jihad against His enemies.

[^1]: Some have said "Safinat" has both masculine and feminine meanings; therefore, it is not restricted to mares.

(فَقالَ إِنِّي أَحْبَبْتُ حُبَّ الْخَيْرِ عَنْ ذِكْرِ رَبِّي).

It is common among Arabs to refer to "Khayl" (horses) as "Khayr" (good/wealth). In a Hadith, it is mentioned that the Noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) said:

"Goodness (Al-Khayr) is tied to the forelocks of horses until the Day of Resurrection." [^1]

Solomon, who was pleased by observing these agile horses ready for Jihad and battle with the enemy, continued to watch them and kept his eyes fixed on them "until they were hidden by the veil [of distance or stable]" (حَتَّى تَوارَتْ بِالْحِجابِ).

The scene was so interesting, beautiful, and exhilarating for a great commander like Solomon that he ordered: "Bring them back to me" (رُدُّوها عَلَيَ). When his officers obeyed this command and brought the horses back, Solomon personally caressed them "and began to pass his hand over their legs and necks" (فَطَفِقَ مَسْحاً بِالسُّوقِ وَ الْأَعْناقِ).

In this way, he both encouraged their trainers and showed appreciation for the animals. It is customary that when one wants to show appreciation for a mount, they stroke its head, face, mane, neck, or legs. Such an expression of affection toward an effective means that aids a person in his lofty goals is not surprising from a great Prophet like Solomon.

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan" under the verse discussed. Some have interpreted "Khayr" in the above verse to mean wealth or abundant wealth, which can align with the previous interpretation, as the instance of wealth here is the horses themselves.

"Tafiqa" (طَفِقَ) (according to grammarians, is one of the verbs of proximity/inception) and means to begin an action.

"Suq" (السُّوقِ) is the plural of "Saq" (leg), and "A'naq" (الْأَعْناقِ) is the plural of "Unuq" (neck). The meaning of the entire sentence is: "Solomon began to wipe and caress their necks and legs."

What was said above in the interpretation of these verses agrees with what some commentators like Fakhr al-Razi have chosen. Among the great Shi'a scholars, part of this interpretation is also found in the words of the renowned and noble scholar Sayyid Murtada. In his book "Tanzih al-Anbiya," when he wishes to negate the improper attributions that some commentators and traditionists have given to Solomon, he says:

"How is it possible for Allah to praise this Prophet at the beginning, then immediately attribute an ugly act to him—that he was busy reviewing horses and forgot his prayer? Rather, the apparent meaning is that his interest in those horses was also by the command of the Lord and His instruction, for Allah has also commanded us to keep and breed horses and prepare them for war against enemies. What is there to prevent a Prophet of Allah from being likewise?" [^1]

The late "Allamah Majlisi" in the book of Prophethood in "Bihar al-Anwar" has statements regarding the interpretation of the above verses, some of which are close in perspective to what we have presented. [^2] In any case, according to this interpretation, no sin was committed by Solomon, the harmony of the verses is not disrupted, and no problem arises that would require justification. [^3]

[^1]: Tanzih al-Anbiya, page 93. [^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, volume 14, page 104. [^3]: According to this interpretation, the pronouns in the phrases "tawarat" and "rudduha" both refer to the swift horses (Al-Safinat al-Jiyad).

Now we turn to other interpretations mentioned by a group of commentators. The most famous among them is that the pronouns in the phrases "tawarat" and "rudduha" both refer to "Shams" (the sun), which is not explicitly mentioned in the text but can be inferred from the expression "al-ashi" (the afternoon). According to this, the meaning of the verses becomes: Solomon was so immersed in watching the horses that the sun set on the horizon and was hidden behind the veil!

Solomon, who became severely angry and upset due to missing his afternoon prayer, called out: "O angels of the Lord! Bring the sun back for me!" This request of Solomon was granted and the "Return of the Sun" (Radd al-Shams) occurred; that is, the sun returned to the horizon. Solomon performed ablution (the meaning of wiping the legs and neck was the ritual of ablution that existed in Solomon's law; of course, sometimes "mash" in Arabic also means washing), then he performed his prayer.

Some uninformed individuals have gone even further and attributed another ugly and improper act to this great Prophet here, saying: the meaning of the phrase "he began to pass his hand over their legs and necks" is that he ordered the legs and necks of the horses to be struck with a sword, or he did it personally, because they were the cause of his forgetting the remembrance of the Lord and his prayer!! Of course, the falsehood of this latter statement is hidden to no one, for the horses committed no sin to be put to the sword by Solomon. If there were a sin, it would be directed at him for being immersed in watching the horses and forgetting other things.

Furthermore, killing the horses is not only a crime but also a waste (israf). How could such an improper act be committed by a Prophet? Therefore, in the narrations found in Islamic sources regarding these verses, this attribution is strongly negated from Solomon.

As for the preceding sentences that speak of forgetting and neglecting the afternoon prayer, that also raises the question: is it possible for an infallible Prophet to forget his obligatory duty? Even if reviewing the horses was another duty of his. Unless, as some have said, the prayer was a supererogatory (nafilah) and recommended prayer, the forgetting of which would not create a problem; but for a supererogatory prayer, the "Return of the Sun" is not necessary.

Beyond these, there are other problems with this interpretation:

  1. The word "Shams" (sun) is not explicitly mentioned in the verses, while the horses (Al-Safinat al-Jiyad) are explicitly mentioned. It is more appropriate for the pronouns to refer to something explicitly stated in the verses.

  2. The expression "because of the remembrance of my Lord" (عَنْ ذِكْرِ رَبِّي) apparently means that the love for these horses stemmed from the remembrance and command of Allah, whereas according to the latter interpretation, the word "an" (عَنْ) must mean "ala" (عَلى), meaning "I preferred the love of horses over the love of my Lord," and this meaning is contrary to the apparent sense (reflect on this).

  3. Most strange of all is the phrase "Bring it back to me" (رُدُّوها عَلَيَ) with that commanding tone. Is it possible for Solomon to ask Allah or His angels to return the sun with such a tone as he uses with his servants?

  4. The issue of "Radd al-Shams" (Return of the Sun), although not impossible before the power of Allah, has clear difficulties that cannot be accepted except in cases where clear evidence exists.

  5. The above verses begin with praise and commendation of Solomon, whereas according to the latter interpretation, these verses end in his blame.

  6. If an obligatory prayer was missed, its justification is difficult, and if it was a supererogatory prayer, what was the necessity for the Return of the Sun?

The only question remaining here is that this interpretation is seen in numerous narrations found in Hadith sources. However, if we examine the chains of transmission (asnad) of these Hadiths, we will realize that none of them have a reliable chain, and they are mostly "mursal" (disconnected) narrations.

Is it not better to set aside these unreliable narrations, leave their knowledge to those qualified, and choose what we understand from the verses with a mind free of preconceptions, thereby becoming free and at ease from various complications?

Commentary

The Severe Trial of Solomon and His Vast Kingdom

These verses recount another part of the story of Solomon (peace be upon him), demonstrating that no matter what level of power a human reaches, they possess nothing of their own; everything comes from Allah. Realizing this truth tears away the veils of arrogance and negligence from one's eyes and makes one aware of their true position in the realm of existence.

The first part of these verses concerns one of the trials Allah ordained for Solomon—a trial accompanied by a "leaving of the better option" (tark al-awla). Following this, Solomon turned toward Allah's threshold and repented for this act.

The concise nature of these verses has once again provided an opportunity for myth-makers and fabulists to construct baseless and illusory stories, attributing matters to this great Prophet that either contradict the foundations of prophethood, conflict with the station of infallibility ('ismah), or fundamentally defy the logic of reason. This itself serves as a test for all researchers of the Quran; had they contented themselves with the literal text of the Quran, no room would remain for these superstitious legends.

In the first verse under discussion, the Quran says: "And We certainly tried Solomon and placed on his throne a body; then he returned [to Allah] in repentance" (وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا سُلَيْمَانَ وَأَلْقَيْنَا عَلَىٰ كُرْسِيِّهِ جَسَدًا ثُمَّ أَنَابَ).

Kursi means a "short-legged throne." It appears that monarchs possessed two types of thrones: one for ordinary occasions with short legs, and another for official and ceremonial sessions with high legs. The former was called kursi and the latter 'arsh.

Jasad refers to a soulless body. According to Raghib in his book Al-Mufradat, its concept is more limited than jism (body), as jasad is generally not applied to anything other than humans (except rarely), whereas jism is more general.

From this verse, it is broadly understood that the subject of Solomon's trial involved a soulless body placed upon his throne before his eyes—something he did not expect and for which he had hoped otherwise. However, the Quran does not provide further details on this matter.

Commentators and narrators have cited various reports and interpretations regarding this, the most plausible and clearest of which is as follows:

Solomon desired to be blessed with brave, noble sons who would assist him in governing the country and especially in Jihad against enemies. Having multiple wives, he said to himself: "I will consort with them so that I may have many sons to help me achieve my goals." However, because he was momentarily negligent and did not say "Insha'Allah" (If Allah wills)—the phrase that expresses a human's reliance on God at all times—no child was born to his wives at that time, except for a deformed child, like a soulless body, which was brought and placed upon his throne! Solomon fell into deep reflection and became distressed as to why he had momentarily neglected Allah and relied upon his own strength. He repented and returned to the threshold of Allah.

Another interpretation worthy of consideration is:

Allah tested Solomon with a severe illness, such that he fell upon his throne like a soulless body. In the Arabic language, it is common to refer to a very weak and ill person as a "body without a soul" (jasad bila ruh). Eventually, he repented, and Allah restored him to his original state (the meaning of anaba being a return to health).

Of course, an objection to this interpretation is that according to this meaning, the text should have been wa alqaynahu (and We cast him), meaning "We cast Solomon upon his throne as a soulless body," whereas this expression does not appear in the verse, and assuming such a hidden pronoun contradicts the apparent meaning (zahir). Furthermore, in this interpretation, the word anaba is taken to mean "returned to health," which also contradicts the apparent meaning.

However, if we take anaba to mean repentance and returning to Allah, it does not harm this interpretation; thus, the only point contrary to the zahir would be the omission of the pronoun in alqaynahu.

As for the ugly, false legends regarding the loss of Solomon's ring, or its theft by one of the devils, and a devil sitting on the throne of government in his place—which appear with great flourish in some books and apparently trace back to the Jewish Talmud and Israeli superstitions—they are incompatible with any reason or logic.

These legends, above all, prove the intellectual decline of those who recount them. Therefore, Islamic researchers, wherever they have mentioned them, have explicitly declared their baselessness. They have stated that neither the station of prophethood nor divine governance is dependent on a ring, nor would Allah ever take this station from a Prophet and allow a devil to take the form of a Prophet, let alone sit in his place for forty days to rule and judge among the people. [1]

In any case, in the following verse, the Quran recounts the matter of Solomon's repentance—mentioned in the final phrase of the previous verse—in more detail, stating: "He said, 'My Lord, forgive me'" (قَالَ رَبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي). "And grant me a kingdom such as shall not belong to anyone after me. Indeed, You are the Bestower" (وَهَبْ لِي مُلْكًا لَا يَنْبَغِي لِأَحَدٍ مِنْ بَعْدِي ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْوَهَّابُ).

Here, two questions arise

  1. Does this request by Solomon suggest stinginess?

In response to this question, commentators have offered many points, a significant portion of which are inconsistent with the apparent meaning of the verses. What seems most appropriate and logical is:

He asked Allah for a type of government accompanied by specific miracles that would distinguish his rule from all other governments. We know that every Prophet had a specific miracle: Moses (peace be upon him) had the staff and the white hand; the fire became cool and safe for Abraham; the miracle of Salih was his specific she-camel; and the miracle of the Prophet of Islam was the Magnificent Quran. Solomon also had a government intertwined with divine miracles—dominion over the winds and the devils, along with many other characteristics.

It is not considered a flaw or deficiency for Prophets to request a specific miracle for themselves to clearly define their status. Therefore, there is no obstacle to others attaining kingdoms larger or more extensive than Solomon's, but they would not possess those specific miraculous characteristics.

The evidence for this is the verses following this one, which reflect the fulfillment of Solomon's request and speak of the subjugation of the wind and the devils. We know this subject was among the unique characteristics of Solomon's rule.

From here, the answer to the second question—which states that according to our belief as Muslims, the government of the Mahdi (may our souls be sacrificed for him) is a global government and certainly more extensive than Solomon's—becomes clear. For despite all the vastness of Imam Mahdi's government and all the distinctions that set it apart from other governments, it differs from Solomon's rule in terms of its specific miraculous features, and Solomon's rule was unique to him.

In short, the issue is not about "more or less," greed, or monopolization; it is about the fact that the perfection of prophethood lies in having specific miracles that distinguish it from the prophethood of other Prophets, and this is what Solomon sought.

In some narrations transmitted through the Ahl al-Bayt from Imam Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him), a very interesting answer is given regarding the question of stinginess.

The Hadith states that one of his companions named Ali ibn Yaqtin asked the Imam (peace be upon him): "Is it permissible for a Prophet of God to be stingy?"

The Imam (peace be upon him) replied: "No."

He asked: "Then why does Solomon say: 'My Lord, forgive me and grant me a kingdom such as shall not belong to anyone after me'? What is the meaning and interpretation of this verse?"

The Imam (peace be upon him) said: "Government is of two types: a government obtained through oppression, conquest, and the coercion of people, and a government that is from Allah, such as the government of the family of Abraham, Talut, and Dhul-Qarnayn. Solomon asked Allah to give him a government such that no one after him could say it was obtained through conquest, oppression, and coercion. Therefore, Allah Almighty subjected the wind to his command, which flowed gently wherever he directed it; it traveled a month's distance in the morning and a month's distance in the evening. And Allah Almighty subjected the devils to him, who built for him and dived [into the sea], and He taught him the language of the birds and established his rule on earth. Thus, at that time and in subsequent times, people knew that his government bore no resemblance to a government chosen by people or obtained through force, conquest, and tyranny."

Ali ibn Yaqtin says: "I then asked, 'What is the interpretation of the statement narrated from the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) who said: "May Allah have mercy on my brother Solomon son of David, how stingy he was!"?'"

The Imam replied: "It has two meanings: First, that he was very 'stingy' (protective) regarding his honor and sanctity, lest anyone say something inappropriate about them. Second, the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) meant that if the verse of the Quran were interpreted as some ignorant people have—that he requested a unique and exclusive kingdom for himself—then he would have to be a stingy man (and this is a sarcasm directed at those ignorant interpreters)." [2]

The subsequent verses, as we mentioned, explain that Allah accepted Solomon's request and granted him a government with unique privileges and great blessings, which can be summarized into five subjects:

  1. Subjugation of the winds as a swift mount, as He says: "So We subjected to him the wind blowing by his command, gently, wherever he directed" (فَسَخَّرْنَا لَهُ الرِّيحَ تَجْرِي بِأَمْرِهِ رُخَاءً حَيْثُ أَصَابَ).

It is certain that a vast and extensive government requires a rapid means of communication so that the head of state can quickly inspect all regions of the country when necessary, and Allah granted this privilege to Solomon.

How the wind was under his command, at what speed it moved, what Solomon and his companions rode upon when moving by the wind, and what factors protected them from falling, changes in air pressure, and other problems—these are matters whose details are not clear to us. We only know that this was among the miracles (khawariq al-'adat) granted to Prophets; it was not an ordinary or common matter, but an extraordinary blessing and a miracle. Such matters are simple before the power of Allah, and there are many things whose essence we know but whose details we do not.

A question arises here: the term rukha’ (gentle/soft) used in this verse seems inconsistent with the term 'asifah (stormy/violent wind) used in verse 81 of Surah Al-Anbiya, where it says: "And to Solomon [We subjected] the wind, blowing violently, proceeding by his command toward the land which We had blessed."

This question can be answered in two ways: First, the description as 'asifah (violent wind) is to express its speed, while the description as rukha’ expresses the orderliness and smoothness of its movement, such that despite the rapid movement, they felt no discomfort—much like modern advanced high-speed vehicles where a passenger sometimes feels as if they are sitting in their own room while moving at a staggering speed. Second, some commentators consider these two verses to refer to two types of wind, both of which Allah placed at Solomon's disposal: one high-speed and one calm.

  1. Another blessing from Allah to Solomon (peace be upon him) was the subjugation of rebellious creatures and placing them at his disposal for constructive work, as the next verse says: "And [We subjected] the devils [of jinn]—every builder and diver" (وَالشَّيَاطِينَ كُلَّ بَنَّاءٍ وَغَوَّاصٍ). Thus, some on land built whatever structures he desired, and some in the sea were engaged in diving. In this way, Allah provided him with a ready force for positive tasks, and the devils, whose nature is rebellion and defiance, became so subjected to him that they were placed on the path of construction and the extraction of precious resources.

Not only in this verse but in several other verses of the Magnificent Quran, it is mentioned that the devils were subjected to Solomon and performed positive activities for him. However, in some verses like the one under discussion and verse 82 of Surah Al-Anbiya, the term "devils" (shayatin) is used, while in verse 12 of Surah Saba, the term "jinn" is used.

As we have said before, "jinn" are beings hidden from our sight but possessing intellect, consciousness, and power; they include both believers and disbelievers. There is no obstacle to them being placed under the command of a Prophet by Allah's leave to engage in useful work. It is also possible that "devils" has a broad meaning encompassing both rebellious humans and others, as the application of "devil" to this broad concept appears in the Magnificent Quran (Al-An'am: 112). Thus, Allah gave Solomon a power that enabled him to make all rebels submit to him.

  1. The third blessing from Allah to Solomon was the restraining of a group of destructive forces. For among the devils, there were individuals who could not be utilized as a useful and constructive force, and there was no choice but for them to be bound so that society would remain safe from their disturbance. As the Quran says in the next verse: "And others bound in shackles" (وَآخَرِينَ مُقَرَّنِينَ فِي الْأَصْفَادِ). Muqarranin is from the root qarn, meaning "conjunction" or "closeness," and here it refers to gathering the hands and feet or the neck in bonds and chains.

Asfad is the plural of safad, meaning fetters or bonds (like the handcuffs and leg irons placed on prisoners). Some have inferred from the phrase muqarranin fil-asfad the "comprehensive shackle" (ghull jami'ah), which was a chain that bound the hands to the neck, consistent with the meaning of muqarranin which implies closeness. It is also possible that this phrase means they were placed in groups within a single bond.

However, the question arises: if "devils" refers to the devils of the jinn, who naturally possess a subtle body, how do shackles, chains, and handcuffs apply to them? Therefore, some have said this expression is a metaphor for their detention and the prevention of their destructive activities. If the reference is to human devils and rebels, then shackles and chains retain their literal meaning.

  1. The fourth blessing from Allah to Solomon was the vast authority that gave him a free hand in granting or withholding, as the next verse says: "[We said], 'This is Our gift, so grant or withhold without account'" (هَذَا عَطَاؤُنَا فَامْنُنْ أَوْ أَمْسِكْ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ). The expression "without account" either indicates that because of your station of justice, Allah has given you such broad authority that you will not be held to account or questioned, or it means that Allah's gift to you is so immense that whatever you give away will not be noticed in the total account.

Some commentators have also limited this expression to the bound devils, meaning "release whomever you wish (and deem appropriate) and keep bound whomever you see fit." However, this meaning seems unlikely as it does not harmonize with the literal sense of the word 'ata'una (Our gift).

  1. The fifth and final blessing of Allah upon Solomon was his spiritual stations, which Allah granted him in light of his worthiness. As the final verse under discussion states: "And indeed, for him is a near station with Us and a good place of return" (وَإِنَّ لَهُ عِنْدَنَا لَزُلْفَىٰ وَحُسْنَ مَآبٍ). This sentence is, in fact, a response to those who have stained the holy threshold of this great Prophet with various unworthy and superstitious attributions—following what appears in the current Torah. Thus, it clears him of all these accusations and honors his station before Allah. Even the expression husna ma'ab (good place of return), which foretells his good end, may be an allusion to the false attribution in the Torah that Solomon, due to marrying idolaters, eventually inclined toward idolatry and even built an idol temple! The Quran, with this expression, draws a line of cancellation through all these illusions and superstitions.

Key Points

1. Truths Taught by the Story of Solomon

Undoubtedly, the Quran's purpose in mentioning the histories of the Prophets is to complete educational programs through the objective reflection of realities in these living accounts. Among the matters that have become objective within the story of Solomon are the following:

A) Possessing a powerful government with vast material resources, an extensive economy, and a brilliant civilization is never inconsistent with spiritual stations and divine and human values. As the above verses, after mentioning all of Solomon's material blessings, state at the end: despite all this, he had a high station and a good end before Allah.

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), it is said: "Have you seen how much kingdom and government Allah gave to Solomon son of David? Yet all these blessings only increased his humility, to the extent that out of humility toward his Lord, he would not even raise his eyes to the sky!" [3]

B) To manage a prosperous country, one needs rapid means of communication, the employment of various forces, the prevention of destructive forces, attention to developmental issues, the production of wealth through the extraction of various resources, and the granting of authority to capable managers—all of which are clearly reflected in this story.

C) Maximum use must be made of available forces; even the "devils" should not be completely eliminated. Rather, those who can be directed and guided should be employed in the correct path, and only that portion which is in no way useful should be restrained.

2. Solomon in the Quran and the Torah

In the depiction the Magnificent Quran provides of this great Prophet in the above verses, he is introduced as a pure, highly valuable, resourceful, and just human being.

In contrast, the current corrupted Torah introduces him (Allah forbid) as a sensualist and a follower of whims with many points of weakness. Surprisingly, in the same book, Solomon's supplications, religious poems, and proverbs are placed alongside the other chapters of the Torah, indicating he was a wise and detached man. This is a strange contradiction that exists within the contents of the current Torah.

For further explanation on this matter, please refer to the detailed discussion in Volume 18 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verses 12 to 14 of Surah Saba (under the title "The Face of Solomon in the Quran and the Current Torah").

We gave [back] his family to him along with others like them, as a mercy from Us and an admonition for those who possess intellect. (43)
وَخُذْ بِيَدِكَ ضِغْثًا فَٱضْرِب بِّهِۦ وَلَا تَحْنَثْ ۗ إِنَّا وَجَدْنَـٰهُ صَابِرًا ۚ نِّعْمَ ٱلْعَبْدُ ۖ إِنَّهُۥٓ أَوَّابٌ (٤٤)
[We told him:] ‘Take a faggot in your hand and then strike [your wife] with it, but do not break [your] oath.’ Indeed, We found him to be patient. What an excellent servant! Indeed he was a penitent [soul]. (44)

Commentary

The Eventful Life of Job and His Station of Patience

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to Solomon and his grandeur, which demonstrated God-given power; this served as a glad tiding for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Muslims of Mecca who were under severe pressure at that time.

In the verses under discussion, the subject is Job (Ayyub), who is the archetype of patience and steadfastness, intended to teach the Muslims of that day—and those of today and tomorrow—the lesson of resistance against the hardships and discomforts of life, to call them to perseverance, and to clarify the praiseworthy end of such patience.

Job is the third prophet whose life story is partially addressed in this Surah. Our great Prophet was tasked with remembering his story and recounting it to the Muslims so they would not fear exhausting hardships and never despair of Allah’s grace and mercy.

The name or story of Job appears in several Surahs of the Quran: in Surah An-Nisa, verse 163, and Surah Al-An'am, verse 84, where only his name is mentioned alongside other prophets, establishing and clarifying his status of prophethood—contrary to the current Torah, which does not count him among the prophets but considers him a wealthy and righteous servant possessing much property and many children.

In Surah Al-Anbiya, verses 83 and 84, a brief explanation of his life is provided, and in the verses under discussion from "Surah Sad," his biography is detailed more extensively than anywhere else in the Quran across four verses.

First, it says: "And remember Our servant Job, when he called upon his Lord, [saying], 'Indeed, Satan has touched me with hardship and torment'" (وَ اذْكُرْ عَبْدَنا أَيُّوبَ إِذْ نادى‌ رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَسَّنِيَ الشَّيْطانُ بِنُصْبٍ وَ عَذابٍ).

"Nusb" (rhyming with usr) and "Nasab" (rhyming with hasad) both mean affliction and evil.

From this verse, firstly, the high station of Job in the presence of Allah is clearly understood through the title "Our servant" (Abdana). Secondly, it contains a cryptic reference to the severe, exhausting afflictions and the immense pain and suffering Job endured.

The full details of this event are not mentioned in the Quran, but in well-known books of Hadith and commentaries, the story is narrated as follows:

Someone asked Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): "Why did the affliction that befell Job occur?" (Perhaps he thought some wrongdoing had been committed by him for which Allah afflicted him).

The Imam gave a detailed response, the summary of which is: "Job did not suffer those great calamities due to ingratitude for blessings; rather, it was the opposite—it was because of his gratitude. For Satan claimed before Allah: 'If You see Job as grateful, it is because of the abundant blessings You have given him. Surely, if these blessings were taken from him, he would never be a grateful servant!' To make Job's sincerity clear to everyone and to set him as an example for the worlds—to be both grateful during 'blessing' and patient during 'hardship'—Allah allowed Satan to gain power over his worldly life. Satan asked Allah for Job's vast wealth, crops, and sheep, as well as his children, to perish; pests and calamities destroyed them in a short time, yet not only did Job's gratitude not diminish, it increased! Then Satan asked to gain power over Job's body, so that he would become so ill that he would writhe in intense pain and suffering, becoming a prisoner to his bed. This too did not diminish his gratitude.

However, an event occurred that broke Job's heart and severely wounded his spirit: a group of monks from the Children of Israel came to visit him and said: 'What sin have you committed to be afflicted with such painful torment?!' Job replied: 'By my Lord, there has been no wrongdoing; I have always been diligent in divine obedience, and for every morsel of food I ate, an orphan or a destitute person was present at my table.'

It is true that Job was more distressed by this reproach from friends than by any other calamity, yet he did not lose the thread of patience, nor did he pollute the pure water of gratitude with ingratitude. He simply turned to the threshold of Allah and uttered the aforementioned words. Since he had successfully passed the divine trials, Allah once again opened the doors of His mercy to this patient and steadfast servant, granting him the lost blessings one after another, and even more, so that everyone might realize the good end of patience, endurance, and gratitude." [^1]

Some great commentators have suggested the possibility that Satan's "torment and suffering" toward Job was through various whisperings. Sometimes he would say: "Your illness has lasted long; your God has forgotten you!" Other times he would say: "What great blessings you had! What health, power, and strength! He took them all from you, yet you still offer Him thanks?!"

[^1]: This narration is quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn from Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim, and the same theme appears in Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Tafsir al-Safi, and other commentaries, as well as A'lam al-Quran with some variations. In the books of the Old Testament, in the Book of Job, similar content is found, though it differs from what is in Islamic sources.

Perhaps this interpretation is because they found it unlikely for Satan to have power over a prophet like Job, or over his life, wealth, and children. However, considering that this power was, firstly, by Allah's command; secondly, limited and temporary; and thirdly, for the purpose of testing this great prophet and elevating his rank, it poses no problem.

In any case, it is said that his distress, suffering, and illness lasted seven years—and according to one narration, eighteen years—reaching a point where even his closest companions and associates left him. Only his wife remained steadfast in her loyalty to Job. This itself is a testament to the loyalty of some wives! Yet, among all the distress and suffering, what pained Job's soul the most was the reproach of enemies. Thus, we read in a Hadith: After Job regained his health and the doors of divine mercy were opened to him, he was asked: "What was your worst pain and suffering?" He replied: "The reproach of enemies!"

Finally, Job emerged healthy from the crucible of this divine trial, and the command of Allah's mercy began here, instructing him: "Strike [the ground] with your foot; this is a [spring for] washing, cool and a drink" (ارْكُضْ بِرِجْلِكَ هذا مُغْتَسَلٌ بارِدٌ وَ شَرابٌ).

"Urkud" is from the root rakd, meaning to strike the foot on the ground, and sometimes it means to run; here it carries the first meaning.

The same God who brought forth the spring of Zamzam from beneath the heel of the infant Ishmael in that dry and scorching desert—and the same God from Whom every movement and stillness, every blessing and gift originates—issued this command regarding Job (عليه السلام). A spring began to gush—a cool, pleasant, and healing spring for both "outer" and "inner" ailments.

Some believe this spring contained a type of mineral water that was both pleasant to drink and had healing effects for diseases. Whatever it was, it was a divine grace and mercy for a patient and steadfast prophet.

"Mughtasal" means water used for washing, and some have interpreted it as the place of washing, but the first meaning seems more correct. In any case, describing the water as "cool" may be an allusion to the specific effect of washing with cold water for the improvement and health of the body, as has been proven in modern medicine.

It is also a subtle indication that the perfection of washing water is that it should be like drinking water in terms of purity and cleanliness! Evidence for this is found in Islamic instructions: "Before you perform Ghusl with water, take a sip of it!" [^1]

The first and most important divine blessing—which was well-being, recovery, and health—returned to Job. Then came the turn for other gifts and blessings to return, and in this regard, the Quran says: "And We gave him [back] his family" (وَ وَهَبْنا لَهُ أَهْلَهُ). "And the like of them with them" (وَ مِثْلَهُمْ مَعَهُمْ). "As a mercy from Us and a reminder for those of understanding" (رَحْمَةً مِنَّا وَ ذِكْرى‌ لِأُولِي الْأَلْبابِ).

[^1]: Wasail al-Shia, Vol. 1, Chapter 13 of the Chapters on the Etiquette of the Bath, Hadith 1.

Regarding how his family returned to him, there are several interpretations. The famous one is that they had died and Allah brought them back to life. However, some have said they had scattered away from him due to his prolonged illness, and when Job regained his health and vitality, they gathered around him once more.

The possibility is also raised that all or some of them had also been afflicted with various illnesses; divine mercy encompassed them as well, they all regained their health, and gathered like moths around the candle of their father's existence.

Adding "the like of them with them" indicates that Allah made his family circle warmer than before and granted him more children.

Although Job's wealth is not explicitly mentioned in these verses, the circumstances indicate that Allah returned it to him in an even more complete form.

It is noteworthy that the end of the above verse cites two reasons for the return of divine favors to Job: one is "mercy from Us upon him," which is personal and is in fact a reward and prize from Allah to this patient servant; the other is "giving a lesson to all possessors of intellect and wisdom throughout history" so that they do not lose the thread of patience and endurance in hardships and severe events, and always remain hopeful of divine mercy.

The only problem remaining for Job was an oath he had taken regarding his wife. He had seen a lapse from her and, in that state of illness, swore that if he gained the strength, he would strike her one hundred times (or less). However, after his recovery, he wanted to forgive her in appreciation of her loyalty and service, but the matter of the oath and the name of Allah was involved.

Allah solved this problem for him as well, as the Quran says: He said, "Take in your hand a bundle of [wheat] stalks (or the like) and strike with it and do not break your oath" (وَ خُذْ بِيَدِكَ ضِغْثاً فَاضْرِبْ بِهِ وَ لا تَحْنَثْ).

"Dighth" refers to a bundle of thin sticks, wheat or barley stalks, or strands of a date cluster, or a bouquet of flowers and the like.

As for what the lapse of Job's wife—whose name according to one narration was "Lia," the daughter of Jacob—was, there is again discussion among commentators.

It is narrated from the famous commentator Ibn Abbas that Satan (or a Satanic person) appeared to his wife in a natural form and said: "I will treat your husband on the condition that when he recovers, he tells me that I was the sole cause of his recovery, and I want no other wage!" His wife, who was deeply distressed by her husband's continued illness, accepted and made this proposal to Job. Job, realizing it was a trap of Satan, became very upset and swore an oath to punish his wife.

Others have said Job sent her to perform a task and she was late; suffering from illness, he became severely agitated and swore such an oath.

In any case, if she was deserving of such punishment from one perspective, she was also deserving of such forgiveness from the perspective of her loyalty throughout her service and nursing.

It is true that striking with a bundle of wheat stalks or date cluster sticks was not the literal fulfillment of his oath, but he did this to maintain respect for the name of Allah and to prevent the spread of law-breaking. This is only permissible in cases where the party is deserving of pardon and one wishes to forgive while maintaining the appearance of the law; otherwise, in cases where forgiveness is not deserved, such an act is never permitted. [^1]

Finally, in the last sentence of the verses under discussion, which is effectively the essence of the beginning and end of this story, He says: "Indeed, We found him patient; an excellent servant [was he]. Indeed, he was one who repeatedly turned [to Us]" (إِنَّا وَجَدْناهُ صابِراً نِعْمَ الْعَبْدُ إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ).

It goes without saying that his prayer to Allah and his request to repel the whisperings of Satan, and the suffering, affliction, and illness, do not contradict the station of patience and endurance—especially after enduring seven years (or eighteen years according to one narration) of pain, illness, poverty, and destitution while remaining grateful.

It is noteworthy that in this sentence, Prophet Job is described with three important attributes which, if found in anyone, make them a perfect human: 1. The station of servanthood (Ubudiyyah). 2. Patience, endurance, and steadfastness. 3. Repeatedly turning back to Allah (Awwab).

Key Points

1- Important Lessons from the Story of Job

Although the entire biography of this patient prophet is contained in only four verses of this Surah, what the Quran has stated inspires important truths:

A: Divine testing is so vast and extensive that even great prophets are tested with the most severe and difficult trials, because the nature of life in this world is built upon this basis, and fundamentally, without difficult trials, the latent talents of humans do not flourish.

B: "Relief after hardship" is another point embedded in this event. When waves of events and calamities pressure a person from every side, one should not only avoid despair and hopelessness but should see it as a sign and a prelude to the opening of the doors of divine mercy, as the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), says: "When hardship reaches its peak, relief is near, and when the rings of affliction tighten, ease and comfort arrive." [^1]

C: From this event, some of the philosophies behind calamities and the difficult events of life become clear, providing an answer to those who consider the existence of pests and calamities as a point of contradiction against the argument from design in the discussion of Monotheism. The existence of these difficult events in the lives of humans—from the great prophets of Allah to ordinary individuals—is a necessity: the necessity of testing, trial, the flourishing of latent talents, and ultimately the evolution of human existence. Thus, it is narrated in some Islamic traditions from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): "The people most severely tested are the prophets, then those who follow them, the best then the next best." [^2] And it is also narrated from the same noble Imam (عليه السلام) that he said: "In Paradise, there is a station that no servant reaches except through the trials and afflictions they encounter." [^3]

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 351. [^2] & [^3]: Safinat al-Bihar, root Bala, Vol. 1, p. 105.

D: This event gives a lesson of patience to all true believers throughout their lives—the same patience and endurance whose end is victory in all fields, and whose result is having a "praiseworthy station" and "high rank" in the presence of the Lord.

E: A trial that occurs for one person is simultaneously a trial for their friends and those around them, to test the level of their sincerity and friendship—to see how loyal they are. When Job lost his wealth, riches, and health, his friends also grew tired and scattered, and both friends and enemies began to reproach and blame him, revealing themselves better than ever before. We saw that Job's suffering from their tongues was greater than any other suffering, for as the famous proverb goes: wounds from spears and swords heal, but the wound that the tongue inflicts on the heart is unhealable!

F: The friends of Allah are not those who remember Him only when blessings arrive; true friends are those who remember Him in "ease" and "hardship," in affliction and blessing, in illness and health, and in poverty and wealth, and the changes of material life do not alter their faith and thoughts. The Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), in that eloquent and passionate sermon describing the qualities of the pious for his sincere friend "Hammam," listed over a hundred attributes for the God-fearing, one of the most important being: "Their souls are the same in times of affliction as they are in times of ease" (and the transformations of life do not change them).

G: This event once again emphasizes the truth that neither the loss of material means and the onset of calamities, hardships, and poverty is a sign of Allah's lack of favor toward a person, nor is having material means a sign of proximity to the presence of the Lord. Rather, a person can be His special servant while possessing all these means, provided they do not become a prisoner to wealth, status, and children, and do not lose the reins of patience upon losing them.

2- "Job" in the Quran and the Torah

We saw the pure face of this great prophet, who is the manifestation of patience and endurance—to the extent that "the patience of Job" is a proverb among all—in the Holy Quran, and how Allah honors him most beautifully at the beginning and end of this story.

Unfortunately, the biography of this great prophet has not remained immune to the tampering of the ignorant or wise enemies, and superstitions have been attached to it from which his holy presence is pure and exempt. Among these is the claim that during his illness, Job's body became infested with worms and became so foul-smelling and putrid that the villagers cast him out of the settlement! Undoubtedly, such a narration is fabricated, even if mentioned within books of Hadith, because the mission of prophets requires that people be able to contact them willingly and eagerly at any time. Anything that causes people's aversion and distance from them—whether it be repulsive diseases, physical defects, or moral harshness—will not be found in them, as it contradicts the philosophy of their mission.

The Holy Quran says regarding the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله): "So by mercy from Allah, [O Muhammad], you were lenient with them. And if you had been rude [in speech] and harsh in heart, they would have disbanded from about you" (Al-Imran: 159). This verse is proof that a prophet must not be such that people scatter from around him.

However, in the Torah, a detailed book about "Job" is found before the "Psalms of David." This book consists of 42 chapters, with detailed discussions in each. In some of these chapters, offensive content is seen, including in the third chapter where it says Job began to complain and lamented greatly, whereas the Quran praises him for his station of patience and endurance.

3- Describing Great Prophets as "Awwab"

In this very Surah (Sad), three great prophets are described as "Awwab": David, Solomon, and Job. In Surah Qaf, verse 32, this description is mentioned for all the people of Paradise: "This is what you were promised—for every Awwab (one who repeatedly turns to Allah) and Hafiz (one who keeps His commands)."

These expressions show that the station of the Awwabin is a high and esteemed one. When we refer to linguistic sources, we find that "Awwab" is from the root awb, meaning to return.

This return—especially considering the form Awwab, which is an intensive form denoting repetition and frequency—indicates that the Awwabin are highly sensitive to factors that distance them from Allah, whether it be the glitter of the material world or the whisperings of the self and devils. If they distance themselves for a moment, they immediately remember and return to Him; if they are heedless for a moment, they think of Him and compensate.

This return can mean returning to divine commands and prohibitions; that is, their reference point and reliance everywhere are His commands, and they return to Him from everywhere.

From the verse "O mountains, repeat [Our] praises with him, and the birds [as well]" (Surah Saba, verse 10), which is about David, another meaning for Awwab is derived: to harmonize and echo. For it says, "O mountains and birds! Echo with David." Therefore, an "Awwab" is one who is in harmony and accord with the laws of creation, divine commands, and the general praise and glorification of the beings of the world. Incidentally, one of the meanings of the name Job (Ayyub) is also Awwab.

Ayahs

وَٱذْكُرْ عِبَـٰدَنَآ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ أُو۟لِى ٱلْأَيْدِى وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ (٤٥)
And remember Our servants, Abraham, Isaac and Jacob, men of strength and insight. (45)
إِنَّآ أَخْلَصْنَـٰهُم بِخَالِصَةٍ ذِكْرَى ٱلدَّارِ (٤٦)
Indeed We purified them with exclusive remembrance of the abode [of the Hereafter]. (46)
وَإِنَّهُمْ عِندَنَا لَمِنَ ٱلْمُصْطَفَيْنَ ٱلْأَخْيَارِ (٤٧)
Indeed they are surely with Us among the elect of the best. (47)
وَٱذْكُرْ إِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَٱلْيَسَعَ وَذَا ٱلْكِفْلِ ۖ وَكُلٌّ مِّنَ ٱلْأَخْيَارِ (٤٨)
And remember Ishmael, Elisha and Dhu’l-Kifl—each [of whom was] among the elect. (48)

Commentary

Six Other Great Prophets

Following the previous verses which provided a detailed account of the lives of "David" and "Solomon," and a briefer description of the life of "Job" and the highlights of this great Prophet's existence, the verses under discussion mention the names of six other great Divine Prophets. They concisely describe their prominent qualities, which serve as models and examples for all humanity.

Interestingly, six different descriptions are mentioned for these six great Prophets, each carrying a specific meaning and concept.

First, addressing the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it says: "And remember Our servants, Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob" (وَ اذْكُرْ عِبادَنا إِبْراهِيمَ وَ إِسْحاقَ وَ يَعْقُوبَ).

The station of "servitude" (ubudiyyah) and being a servant is the first description mentioned for them, and truly, everything is encompassed within it. Servitude to Allah means absolute dependence on Him; it means having no will of one's own against His will, and submitting to His command in all circumstances.

Servitude to Allah means being independent of anyone other than Him, being indifferent to everything besides Allah, and fixing one's eyes only upon His grace. This is the very peak of human perfection and his highest honor and glory.

Then it adds: "possessors of strength and vision" (أُولِي الْأَيْدِي وَ الْأَبْصارِ).

What a remarkable expression! "Possessors of hands and eyes!" Aydi is the plural of yad (hand), and absar is the plural of basar, meaning "eye" and "insight."

To advance his goals, a human needs two powers: the power of perception and discernment, and the power of action and implementation. In other words, one must seek help from "knowledge" and "power" to reach the goal.

Allah has described these Prophets as possessing "strong perception, discernment, and insight" and "sufficient strength and power" to carry out the task.

They were not ill-informed individuals; the level of their gnosis was high, and the extent of their awareness regarding Allah's religion, the secrets of creation, and the mysteries of life was significant.

In terms of will, determination, and the power of action, they were not weak, listless, or helpless individuals; rather, they were people of will, great power, and possessed decisive and iron-clad determination.

This is a model for all wayfarers of the path of Truth: that after the station of servitude to Allah, they should arm themselves with these two sharp weapons.

From what we have said, it becomes clear that "hands" and "eyes" here do not refer to the two specific physical organs, for there are many who possess these two organs but lack sufficient understanding and intellect, the power of decision-making, or the ability to act. Rather, it is an allusion to the two qualities of "knowledge and power."

In the fourth description of them, He says: "Indeed, We purified them with a special purity" (إِنَّا أَخْلَصْناهُمْ بِخالِصَةٍ). "Which was the remembrance of the Abode [of the Hereafter]" (ذِكْرَى الدَّارِ) [1]. Yes, they were constantly mindful of the other world. Their horizon of vision was not limited to the few days of this world's life and its pleasures; they saw the eternal abode with its endless blessings beyond this fleeting life and always strove and worked for it.

Therefore, the meaning of "the Abode" (al-Dar), mentioned here in an absolute sense, is the Abode of the Hereafter—as if no other abode truly exists, and whatever is besides it is merely a passage toward it! Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that "Abode" here refers to the worldly abode, and the expression "remembrance of the Abode" refers to the good name left behind by these Prophets in this world. However, this possibility—especially considering the absolute nature of "the Abode"—seems very remote and does not align well with the word dhikra (remembrance).

Some have also suggested it refers to a good name and beautiful mention in the Hereafter, which also seems unlikely.

In any case, others might occasionally remember the Abode of the Hereafter, especially when one of their friends passes away or when they attend a funeral or memorial service for a loved one. But this remembrance is not "pure" (khalis); it is mixed with the remembrance of the world. However, the men of God have a pure, deep, constant, and continuous focus on the other Abode, as if it is always present before their eyes, and the expression khalisah (pure/special) in the verse refers to this very fact.

Their fifth and sixth descriptions are what follow in the next verse: He says: "And indeed, they are, in Our sight, among the chosen and the outstanding" (وَ إِنَّهُمْ عِنْدَنا لَمِنَ الْمُصْطَفَيْنَ الْأَخْيارِ) [2]. Their faith and righteous deeds caused Allah to choose them from among His servants and honor them with the office of Prophethood and Messengership. Their virtue reached such a level that they earned the title of "the outstanding" (al-akhyar) in an absolute sense. Their thoughts are good, their character is good, and their actions, programs, and entire lives are good; "whatever all the beautiful ones possess, they possess alone." For this reason, some commentators have used this expression—where Allah calls them "the outstanding" without any condition or qualification—to establish the station of infallibility ('ismah) for the Prophets. For whenever a human is "absolutely good," he must certainly be infallible [3].

The expression "in Our sight" ('indana) is a very meaningful one. it indicates that their being chosen and their goodness is not merely in the eyes of people—who might allow various forms of negligence or oversight in their evaluations—but rather their description with these two attributes is established "in Our sight," having been performed with complete precision and an evaluation of both their inner and outer selves.

After referring to the prominent stations of the three Prophets above, it becomes the turn of three other great Prophets. He says: "And remember Ishmael, Elisha, and Dhul-Kifl, and all are among the outstanding" (وَ اذْكُرْ إِسْماعِيلَ وَ الْيَسَعَ وَ ذَا الْكِفْلِ وَ كُلٌّ مِنَ الْأَخْيارِ).

Each of them was a model and example of patience, steadfastness, and obedience to Allah's command. Especially Ishmael, who was prepared to sacrifice his life in His path, for which reason he was called "the Sacrifice of Allah" (Dhabih Allah). He collaborated extensively with his father Abraham in building the House of the Kaaba, warming this great center, and in other missions. Paying attention to their lives is inspiring for the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and all Muslims.
