Studying the lives of such great men gives direction to human life, revives the spirit of piety, sacrifice, and altruism within them, and makes them resilient against hardships and difficult events.

The expression "all are among the outstanding" (kullun min al-akhyar), considering that this same description (al-akhyar) was used exactly for "Abraham," "Isaac," and "Jacob" as their final attribute, may indicate that these three Prophets also possessed all the qualities of the previous three. This is because "absolute goodness" has a broad meaning that encompasses Prophethood, mindfulness of the Hereafter, the station of servitude, and knowledge and power.

Among these three Prophets, "Ishmael" is the most famous and well-known. As for "Elisha" (al-Yasa'), whose name appears twice in the Holy Quran (here and in Surah Al-An'am, verse 86), the Quranic expression regarding him shows that he was one of the great Divine Prophets and among those of whom He says: "And each We preferred over the worlds" (Al-An'am: 86).

Some believe he is the same as "Joshua son of Nun," the famous Prophet of the Israelites, where the "Alif and Lam" were added and the "Shin" was changed to "Sin." The addition of "Alif and Lam" to a non-Arabic name (in this case, Hebrew) is not something new, just as the Arabs know "Alexander" as "Al-Iskandar."

Others consider it an Arabic word derived from yasa' (a present-tense verb from the root wus'at), and after it took on a nominal form, the "Alif and Lam"—which are characteristics of a noun—were added to it.

The verse in Surah Al-An'am shows that he is from the lineage of Abraham, but it does not clarify whether he was among the Prophets of the Israelites or not.

In the Torah, in the Book of Kings, his name is recorded as "Elisha" son of "Shaphat." The meaning of Elisha in Hebrew is "savior" and the meaning of Shaphat is "judge."

Some have identified him with "Khidr," but there is no clear evidence for this. The fact that some consider him to be the same as "Dhul-Kifl" contradicts the explicit wording of the verse under discussion, as it conjoins Dhul-Kifl to Elisha. In any case, he is a high-ranking and very steadfast Prophet, and this is sufficient for us to draw inspiration from his life.

As for "Dhul-Kifl," the famous view is that he was a Prophet, and the mention of his name alongside the names of Prophets in Surah Al-Anbiya, verse 85, after the names of Ishmael and Idris, is evidence of this.

Some believe he was one of the Prophets of the Israelites and consider him the son of Job, whose original name was "Bishr," "Bashir," or "Sharaf." Some identify him as "Ezekiel," with Dhul-Kifl being chosen as his title [4].

Regarding why he was named "Dhul-Kifl"—considering that kifl means both "portion/share" and "sponsorship/guaranty"—various possibilities have been suggested:

Sometimes it is said: because Allah granted him an abundant portion (kifl) of His reward and mercy, he was named "Dhul-Kifl," meaning "possessor of the ample portion."

Sometimes it is said: because he had committed to standing in worship at night, fasting during the day, and never becoming angry while judging, and he remained faithful to this covenant, this title was given to him.

And sometimes it is said: because he took charge of (kafalat) a group of the Prophets of the Israelites and protected their lives against the tyrannical king of the time, he was called by this name.

In any case, the amount of his life story available to us today is evidence of his steadfastness in the path of obedience and servitude to Allah and resistance against tyrants. It is a model for our today and tomorrow, even if a precise judgment regarding the details of their lives cannot be made due to the passage of time.

[1]: "Remembrance of the Abode" (dhikra al-dar) might be the predicate of a hidden subject, estimated as "it is the remembrance of the Abode," or it might be an "appositive" (badal) for khalisah. [2]: Mustafayn (with a fatha on the fa) is the plural of Mustafa. Originally it was Mustafayeen; the first ya was deleted, becoming Mustafayn. [3]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, vol. 26, p. 217. [4]: A'lam al-Quran, Tafsir Qurtubi, Tafsir Ruh al-Bayan, and Tafsir al-Mizan have each referred to parts of the above information.

Ayahs

هَـٰذَا ذِكْرٌ ۚ وَإِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ لَحُسْنَ مَـَٔابٍ (٤٩)
This is a Reminder, and indeed the Godwary have a good destination: (49)
جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ مُّفَتَّحَةً لَّهُمُ ٱلْأَبْوَٰبُ (٥٠)
the Gardens of Eden, whose gates will be flung open for them. (50)
مُتَّكِـِٔينَ فِيهَا يَدْعُونَ فِيهَا بِفَـٰكِهَةٍ كَثِيرَةٍ وَشَرَابٍ (٥١)
Reclining therein [on couches], they will call for abundant fruits and drinks, (51)
۞ وَعِندَهُمْ قَـٰصِرَٰتُ ٱلطَّرْفِ أَتْرَابٌ (٥٢)
and there will be with them maidens of restrained glances, of a like age. (52)
هَـٰذَا مَا تُوعَدُونَ لِيَوْمِ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٥٣)
This is what you are promised on the Day of Reckoning. (53)
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَرِزْقُنَا مَا لَهُۥ مِن نَّفَادٍ (٥٤)
This is Our provision, which will never be exhausted. (54)

Commentary

This Promise is for the Pious

From here, another section of the verses of this Surah begins, comparing the pious and the God-fearing with the rebellious transgressors, and describing the fate of both groups on the Day of Resurrection; overall, it completes the discussions of the previous verses.

First, as a summary of the accounts of the previous prophets and the instructive points of their lives, He says: "This is a reminder" (هذا ذِكْرٌ) [^1]. Indeed, the purpose of recounting segments of their glorious history was not storytelling; the goal was remembrance and reminder, just as this issue was emphasized from the beginning of this Surah: "ص وَ الْقُرْآنِ ذِي الذِّكْرِ" (Sad. By the Quran containing the reminder).

The goal is to awaken thoughts, raise the level of knowledge and awareness, and increase the power of resistance and steadfastness in the Muslims for whom these verses were revealed. [^2] Then, He takes the matter out of its specific form—the description of the lives of the prophets—and gives it a general form, discussing the fate of the pious in general, saying:

"And indeed, for the righteous is a good place of return" (وَ إِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ لَحُسْنَ مَآبٍ) [^3]

[^1]: Some commentators have said in the interpretation of this sentence that it refers to the "beautiful mention" (good reputation) of the past prophets.

[^2]: A group of commentators have considered "هذا ذِكْرٌ" as an indication that what was stated regarding the past prophets was a mention of good and beautiful praise for them, and the subsequent verses recount their ranks in the Hereafter; however, this meaning seems unlikely. Rather, the apparent meaning of the verses is general, as we stated above.

[^3]: "Ma'ab" means a place of return, and the addition of "Husn" (good/beautiful) to "Ma'ab" is of the type of adding an attribute to the noun it describes.

After this short and concise sentence that sketches their good state in brief, using the method of "summary followed by detail" which is the method of the Quran, He proceeds to explain it, saying: "Their return is to the Gardens of Eternity (Eden), whose gates are opened for them" (جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ مُفَتَّحَةً لَهُمُ الْأَبْوابُ) [^1]. "Jannat" refers to the gardens of Paradise, and "Adn" (on the weight of 'Adl') means stability and permanence; a "Ma'dan" (mine) is called such because metals and precious materials are stable there. In any case, this expression here refers to the eternal nature of the gardens of Paradise.

The expression "مُفَتَّحَةً لَهُمُ الْأَبْوابُ" (the gates opened for them) indicates that even the trouble of opening the doors does not exist for the people of Paradise; it is as if Paradise is waiting for them, and when its eye falls upon them, it opens its arms and invites them inside!

Then, He describes the particular tranquility and honor of the people of Paradise in this way: "While they are reclining therein on couches, calling therein for abundant fruit and drink" (مُتَّكِئِينَ فِيها يَدْعُونَ فِيها بِفاكِهَةٍ كَثِيرَةٍ وَ شَرابٍ) [^2]. Whenever they desire it, it is immediately present before them. Is it immediately brought before them by the heavenly servants, or

[^1]: "جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ" is a substitute (Badal) or an explanatory apposition (Atf al-Bayan) for "Ma'ab".

[^2]: The pronoun "fiha" in both instances refers back to "جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ", and the description of "fakiha" (fruit) as "kathira" (abundant) is evidence for describing "sharab" (drink) with this attribute as well. "Mutaki'in" (reclining) is a state (Hal) for the pronoun in "lahum"; meaning they are settled in the eternal Paradise whose doors are open, while reclining on thrones, and while ordering various fruits and types of drinks to be brought.

is their mere will sufficient for its presence? Both possibilities exist.

The emphasis on "fruit" and "drink" may indicate that the primary food of the people of Paradise is fruit, even though other foods also exist there according to the explicit verses of the Quran.

Just as the best and healthiest food for humans in this world is also fruit! The expression "kathira" (abundant) refers to the various types of heavenly fruits, just as its "pure drink" (sharabun tahur) also has diverse forms mentioned in various verses of the Quran.

After that, He mentions the pure heavenly spouses, saying: "And with them are spouses of modest gaze, well-matched (in age)" (وَ عِنْدَهُمْ قاصِراتُ الطَّرْفِ أَتْرابٌ). "Tarf" means the eyelid, and sometimes it is used to mean "a glance." Describing the heavenly women as "qasirat al-tarf" (those with restricted glances) indicates that they have fixed their eyes only on their own husbands; they love only them and do not think of anyone else, which is among the greatest merits of a spouse. Some commentators have also interpreted it as the state of "languid eyes," which is a specifically attractive state; there is no obstacle to combining these two meanings.

"Atrab" means "of the same age," which is another description for the heavenly women in relation to their husbands, because age compatibility increases the attraction between two spouses; or it is a description for the women themselves, that they are all of the same age and young. [^1]

[^1]: "Atrab" is the plural of "Tirb".

In the final verses under discussion, He refers to all seven great blessings of the people of Paradise mentioned in the previous verses, saying: "This is what you are promised for the Day of Account" (هذا ما تُوعَدُونَ لِيَوْمِ الْحِسابِ). A promise that is unfailing and exhilarating, a promise from the Great Lord.

And to emphasize the eternity of these bounties, He adds: "Indeed, this is Our provision; for it there is no depletion" (إِنَّ هذا لَرِزْقُنا ما لَهُ مِنْ نَفادٍ) [^1]. Therefore, the grief of decline and destruction, which falls like a dark shadow over the blessings of this world, does not exist there. It is constantly supported by the grace of the abundant divine treasures, and there is no limit to it, nor does any decrease appear in it, because the Will of Allah has been attached to it.

[^1]: "Nafad" means "annihilation" and destruction, and the "Lam" in "larizquna" is for emphasis.

Ayahs

هَـٰذَا ۚ وَإِنَّ لِلطَّـٰغِينَ لَشَرَّ مَـَٔابٍ (٥٥)
This [will be for the righteous], and as for the rebellious there will surely be a bad destination: (55)
جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَوْنَهَا فَبِئْسَ ٱلْمِهَادُ (٥٦)
hell, which they shall enter, an evil resting place. (56)
هَـٰذَا فَلْيَذُوقُوهُ حَمِيمٌ وَغَسَّاقٌ (٥٧)
[They will be told, ‘This is scalding water and pus; let them taste it, (57)
وَءَاخَرُ مِن شَكْلِهِۦٓ أَزْوَٰجٌ (٥٨)
and other kinds [of torments] resembling it.’ (58)
هَـٰذَا فَوْجٌ مُّقْتَحِمٌ مَّعَكُمْ ۖ لَا مَرْحَبًۢا بِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ صَالُوا۟ ٱلنَّارِ (٥٩)
[The leaders of the faithless will be told,] ‘This is a group [of your followers] plunging [into hell] along with you.’ [They will repond,] ‘May wretchedness be their lot! For they will enter the Fire.’ (59)
قَالُوا۟ بَلْ أَنتُمْ لَا مَرْحَبًۢا بِكُمْ ۖ أَنتُمْ قَدَّمْتُمُوهُ لَنَا ۖ فَبِئْسَ ٱلْقَرَارُ (٦٠)
They will say, ‘No, may wretchedness be your lot! You prepared this [hell] for us. What an evil abode!’ (60)
قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا مَن قَدَّمَ لَنَا هَـٰذَا فَزِدْهُ عَذَابًا ضِعْفًا فِى ٱلنَّارِ (٦١)
They will say, ‘Our Lord! Whoever has prepared this for us, double his punishment in the Fire!’ (61)

Commentary

And This is the Punishment of the Transgressors!

In the preceding verses, the sevenfold blessings and the unstinting favors of the Lord for the righteous were enumerated. In the verses under discussion, utilizing the method of contrast and comparison—which the Quran frequently employs—the grim fate and the various punishments of the tyrants and those who rebel against Allah are recounted.

First, it states: "This is what has been mentioned as the rewards for the 'pious,' but for the transgressors, there is the most evil destination and place of return!" (هذا وَ إِنَّ لِلطَّاغِينَ لَشَرَّ مَآبٍ) ^1. While the pious have a "goodly return" (husna ma’ab), these people have an "evil return" (sharra ma’ab) and a sinister fate.

Then, using the method of brevity followed by detail, it elaborates on this concise statement, saying: "This evil return and wretched destination is Hell itself, which they shall enter and in whose fire they shall burn; and what an evil bed is the fire of Hell!" (جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَوْنَها فَبِئْسَ الْمِهادُ) [^2]

[^2]: "جهنم" (Hell) is an appositive (atf-e bayan) or a substitute (badal) for "evil return," and "يصلونها" (they shall enter it) describes its state (hal).

It seems the phrase "يصلونها" (they enter Hell and burn in its fire) is meant to clarify that no one should imagine they only see Hell from a distance or stand beside it; no, they are cast into its depths. Furthermore, no one should delude themselves into thinking they will grow accustomed to or find affinity with the fire of Hell; rather, they shall burn in it perpetually.

"مهاد"—as we have mentioned previously—refers to a bed spread out for sleep and rest; it is also applied to a child's cradle. A bed is a place of rest and must be suitable and pleasant in every respect; but what will be the condition of those whose bed is the fire of Hell?!

Then, it addresses other types of their torments, saying: "This is boiling water and fluid of pus; so let them taste it!" (هذا فَلْيَذُوقُوهُ حَمِيمٌ وَ غَسَّاقٌ) ^1. "حميم" (Hamim) refers to scalding, boiling water, which is one of the drinks of the denizens of Hell, contrasting with the various types of "pure wine" (sharabun tahur) mentioned for the people of Paradise in the previous verses.

"غساق" (Ghassaq) is derived from the root ghasaq (rhyming with ramaq), meaning the intense darkness of the night. Ibn Abbas interpreted it as an extremely cold drink (which, due to its intense frigidity, burns and wounds the human interior). However, there is nothing in the root meaning of this word that explicitly denotes this, except that its contrast with Hamim (scalding water) may have been the basis for such an inference.

^1: This sentence was originally "هذا حميم و غساق فليذوقوه," where the phrase "فليذوقوه" (let them taste it) was placed between the subject and predicate for emphasis. Some commentators also consider it possible that "هذا" is the predicate of a hidden subject, just as "حميم و غساق" are, estimated as: "The punishment is this, so let them taste it; this is boiling water and pus." However, the first possibility seems more subtle and elegant.

"Raghib" in Al-Mufradat interprets it as the secretions that ooze from the skin of the denizens of Hell (and their bodily wounds). Likely, its dark color is the reason for applying this term to it, for the product of that scorching fire is nothing but a mass of burnt limbs with black exudations! In any case, it appears from some accounts that Ghassaq has an extremely foul and offensive odor that distresses everyone.

Others have interpreted it as a type of punishment known only to Allah, because they committed grave sins and injustices known only to Allah, and thus their punishment must be likewise. Just as the pious people of Paradise performed good deeds known only to Allah, they were promised rewards that no one but Allah knows: "فَلا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ ما أُخْفِيَ لَهُمْ مِنْ قُرَّةِ أَعْيُنٍ" (No soul knows what delights of the eye are kept hidden for them) (As-Sajdah: 17).

Again, it points to other types of their painful torments, saying: "And besides these, there are other punishments of a similar kind" (وَ آخَرُ مِنْ شَكْلِهِ أَزْواجٌ) ^1. "شكل" (shakl) means like or similar, and "أزواج" (azwaj) means types and varieties. This is a brief reference to other kinds of torment similar to the previous ones, which are stated here concisely and perhaps cannot be described or understood by those imprisoned in this material world.

This is, in fact, the counterpart to "بِفاكِهَةٍ كَثِيرَةٍ" (with abundant fruit) in the previous verses, which referred to the various types of heavenly blessings and fruits. In any case, this similarity may be in intensity and distress, or in all aspects.

Then, it presents their final punishment, which is evil companions with tongues full of reproach, saying: When the leaders of misguidance enter Hell and see with their own eyes that their followers are also being brought toward Hell, they say to one another: "This is a troop rushing in with you" (هذا فَوْجٌ مُقْتَحِمٌ مَعَكُمْ) ^1. "No welcome for them!" (لا مَرْحَباً بِهِمْ). "They shall surely burn in the Fire" (إِنَّهُمْ صالُوا النَّارِ).

The phrase "هذا فَوْجٌ مُقْتَحِمٌ مَعَكُمْ," based on the context of the subsequent phrases and verses, is the speech of the leaders of misguidance. When they see their followers prepared to enter Hell, they say to one another: "These too will be with you." Some commentators also consider it the address of angels to the ringleaders of disbelief and rebellion, but the first meaning seems more appropriate.

"مرحبا" (Marhaba) is a word said when welcoming a guest, and "لا مرحبا" (La Marhaba) is its opposite. This word is a noun derived from the root ruhb, meaning spaciousness of place; i.e., "Enter, for you have come into a spacious and suitable place." Its equivalent in Persian is the phrase "Khosh amadid" (Welcome).

"مقتحم" (Muqtahim) is from the root iqtiham, meaning to enter into a severe and fearsome task...

^1: This sentence has an omitted part, estimated as: "The leaders of misguidance say to one another: This is a troop rushing in with you."

...and it is often applied to entering into matters without prior study or thought. This expression indicates that the followers of misguidance enter the intense and fearsome fire of Hell without reflection or thought, purely out of passion and blind imitation.

In any case, this voice reaches the followers, and they become deeply enraged by the "un-welcoming" of the leaders of misguidance. Turning toward them, "they say: Nay, it is you for whom there is no welcome, for it was you who proposed this painful torment to us and prepared it for us; what an evil resting place is Hell!" (قالُوا بَلْ أَنْتُمْ لا مَرْحَباً بِكُمْ أَنْتُمْ قَدَّمْتُمُوهُ لَنا فَبِئْسَ الْقَرارُ).

The final phrase (فَبِئْسَ الْقَرارُ) is, in truth, the counterpart to "جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ" (Gardens of Eternity) mentioned regarding the pious, pointing out that the great tragedy is that Hell is not a temporary place, but a permanent "resting place" (qarar)! The followers' aim with this expression is to say: "Whatever this is, it has the merit that you, the leaders of misguidance, are also partners with us in this matter, and this brings some healing to our hearts," or it points to the fact that "your crime against us, O leaders, was a truly massive crime, for Hell is not a temporary station but our permanent abode."

Yet, despite this, the followers are not satisfied with these words, because they consider the leaders of misguidance—who were the primary cause of the crime—more deserving than themselves. Therefore, they turn to the threshold of Allah and "say: Our Lord! Whoever brought this upon us, increase for him a double punishment in the fire of Hell" (قالُوا رَبَّنا مَنْ قَدَّمَ لَنا هذا فَزِدْهُ عَذاباً ضِعْفاً فِي النَّارِ).

One punishment for their own misguidance, and one punishment for misleading us. This verse is similar to what appears in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 38: "رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ أَضَلُّونا فَآتِهِمْ عَذاباً ضِعْفاً مِنَ النَّارِ" (Our Lord! These misled us, so give them a double punishment of the Fire!). Although the continuation of that same verse in Surah Al-A'raf says, "For each there is double" (since the followers were also the executive force for the leaders, and the grounds for misguidance and corruption were prepared by them—for if the masses do not heat the tyrants' oven, the tyrants would have no power to do anything). Nevertheless, there is no doubt that the punishment of the leaders is heavier by degrees, even if both have double punishment.

Yes, this is the end of those who made pacts of friendship with one another and pledged allegiance in the way of deviation and misguidance; when they see the grim results of their actions, they rise in litigation, enmity, and cursing one another.

It is noteworthy that in these verses, the mention of the blessings for the pious has more variety than the mention of the penalties and torments for the transgressors (in the first part, seven favors are mentioned, and in the second part, five punishments). This is perhaps because Allah's mercy precedes His wrath: "O He whose mercy precedes His wrath!" (Ya man sabaqat rahmatuhu ghadabah).

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ مَا لَنَا لَا نَرَىٰ رِجَالًا كُنَّا نَعُدُّهُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَشْرَارِ (٦٢)
And they will say, ‘Why is it that we do not see [here] men whom we used to count among the bad ones, (62)
أَتَّخَذْنَـٰهُمْ سِخْرِيًّا أَمْ زَاغَتْ عَنْهُمُ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ (٦٣)
ridiculing them, or do [our] eyes miss them [here]?’ (63)
إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ لَحَقٌّ تَخَاصُمُ أَهْلِ ٱلنَّارِ (٦٤)
That is indeed a true account of the contentions of the inmates of the Fire. (64)

Commentary

The Dispute of the Inhabitants of Hell!

These verses continue the discussion regarding the dialogues of the people of Hell that were mentioned in the previous verses, describing another one of their conversations which reflects a deep, agonizing regret and a soul-crushing spiritual torment.

It says: "When the leaders of misguidance look around themselves in Hell, they say: 'Why do we not see men whom we used to count among the wicked?'" (وَ قالُوا ما لَنا لا نَرى‌ رِجالًا كُنَّا نَعُدُّهُمْ مِنَ الْأَشْرارِ).

Indeed, individuals such as the "Abu Jahls" and "Abu Lahabs," when they see no trace of the likes of "Ammar Yasir," "Khabbab," "Suhaib," and "Bilal" in Hell, they come to their senses and ask one another: "So what happened to these people?"

We considered them a handful of agitators, corrupters on earth, wicked, and ruffians who had risen to disturb the peace of society and destroy the glories of our ancestors; it seems our judgment was entirely wrong!

"Did we take them in mockery, or have [our] eyes missed them?" (أَتَّخَذْناهُمْ سِخْرِيًّا أَمْ زاغَتْ عَنْهُمُ الْأَبْصارُ) [1]. Yes, we used to mock these great and dignified men, labeling them as "wicked," and sometimes we even considered them lower than that—viewing them as insignificant individuals who were not worth noticing at all. But it has become clear that whims, desires, ignorance, and pride had cast a heavy veil over our eyes; they were those drawn near to Allah's court, and now Paradise is their abode.

A group of commentators has suggested another possibility in interpreting the above verse: The issue of "mockery" refers to the state of the world, while the phrase "or have [our] eyes missed them" refers to the state of Hell—meaning that here, our short-sighted eyes cannot see them amidst these flames of smoke and fire. However, the first meaning seems more correct.

It is noteworthy that one of the factors preventing the perception of realities is not taking matters seriously and mocking or joking with truths. One must always approach the investigation of realities with a serious resolve so that the truth may become clear.

Then, as a summary of the dialogues that take place among the inhabitants of Hell, and as an emphasis on what has passed, He says: "Indeed, that is a reality—the quarreling of the people of the Fire" (إِنَّ ذلِكَ لَحَقٌّ تَخاصُمُ أَهْلِ النَّارِ) [1]. The inhabitants of Hell were also plagued by disputes and quarrels in this world, and a spirit of aggression, conflict, and contention dominated them; every day they were embroiled and clashing with someone. In the Resurrection, which is the stage for the manifestation of hidden secrets, what they held within becomes apparent, and in Hell, they fall upon one another. Yesterday's friends become today's enemies, and yesterday's followers become today's opponents. Only the path of faith and Monotheism (Tawhid) is the path of unity and sincerity in both this world and the next.

Interestingly, the people of Paradise recline on couches and thrones, engaged in friendly conversations, as mentioned in various verses of the Quran, while the people of Hell are in a state of war and strife. The former is a great blessing, and the latter is a painful punishment!

Key Point

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is narrated that he said to one of his companions: "Allah has mentioned you, the followers of the school of the Ahl al-Bayt, in the Quran where your enemies in the fire of Hell say: 'Why do we not see here men whom we used to count among the wicked? Did we take them in mockery, or did they not come to our sight due to extreme insignificance?' By Allah, you are the intended meaning of those 'men' whom a group considers to be among the wicked, but by Allah, you are joyful and delighted in Paradise, while the inhabitants of Hell search for you in Jahannam." [2]

[1]: It is noteworthy that "zaaghat," which comes from the root "zaygh" meaning deviation from truth and correctness, is attributed here to the "eyes" rather than the owners of the eyes, and this is to express exaggeration in the matter.

[1]: "Takhāsumu ahli al-nār" (the quarreling of the people of the Fire) serves as an explanation for "dhalika" (that).

[2]: "Rawdat al-Kafi," as cited in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 467.

Ayahs

قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ مُنذِرٌ ۖ وَمَا مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ٱلْوَٰحِدُ ٱلْقَهَّارُ (٦٥)
Say, ‘I am just a warner, and there is no god except Allah, the One, the All-paramount, (65)
رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْغَفَّـٰرُ (٦٦)
the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, the All-mighty, the All-forgiver.’ (66)
قُلْ هُوَ نَبَؤٌا۟ عَظِيمٌ (٦٧)
Say, ‘It is a great prophesy, (67)
أَنتُمْ عَنْهُ مُعْرِضُونَ (٦٨)
of which you are disregardful. (68)
مَا كَانَ لِىَ مِنْ عِلْمٍۭ بِٱلْمَلَإِ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰٓ إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ (٦٩)
I have no knowledge of the Supernal Elite when they contend. (69)
إِن يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ إِلَّآ أَنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٧٠)
All that is revealed to me is that I am just a manifest warner.’ (70)

Commentary

I Am Only a Warner!

Since all the preceding discussions—both those speaking of the painful punishment of the people of Hell and those discussing the worldly torment of sinful nations of the past—serve as a warning and threat to the polytheists, the rebellious, and the oppressors, the verses under discussion pursue this same matter, saying: "Say: I am only a warner!" (قُلْ إِنَّما أَنَا مُنْذِرٌ).

It is true that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is also a bearer of glad tidings, and the verses of the Holy Quran speak to both meanings; however, since glad tidings are for the believers and warning is for the polytheists and the corrupt, and here the discourse is addressed to the latter group, the emphasis is placed solely on warning.

Then He adds: "And there is no god but Allah, the One, the Subduer" (وَ ما مِنْ إِلهٍ إِلَّا اللَّهُ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ).

The emphasis on His being "the Subduer" (al-Qahhar) is also for this purpose: so that no one becomes deluded by His grace, nor considers themselves safe from His wrath, nor plunges into the whirlpool of disbelief and sin.

Immediately, as a statement of proof for the Oneness of Divinity and the worship of the Lord, He adds: "The Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, the All-Mighty, the All-Forgiving" (رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما بَيْنَهُمَا الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفَّارُ). In reality, three attributes of Allah's attributes are mentioned in this verse, each of which serves to prove a specific purpose.

First is the issue of His "Lordship" (Rububiyyah) over the entire realm of existence. He is the Owner of all this world—an Owner who manages and nurtures them. Only He who is such is worthy of "servitude" (Ubudiyyah), not idols that do not possess even an atom's weight of anything for themselves.

Second is the issue of His "Might" (Izzah). We know that "al-Aziz" (the All-Mighty), linguistically, refers to one whom no one can overcome, and whatever He wills must come to pass; in other words, He is always the Victor and never the vanquished. For one who is such, how is it possible to flee from the grasp of His power? And how is escape from His punishment feasible?

The third description is the station of His "Forgiveness" (Ghaffariyyah) and the abundance of His pardoning, which opens the doors of return to the sinners and rains His mercy upon them, so they do not imagine that if He is "the Subduer" and "the All-Mighty," it means the doors of mercy and repentance are closed to His servants.

In truth, one is an expression of "fear" (khawf) and the other an expression of "hope" (raja'), for without the balance of these two states, human perfection is impossible; one either falls victim to pride and negligence or sinks into the whirlpool of despair and hopelessness.

In other words, describing Him as "the All-Mighty" and "the All-Forgiving" is another proof of His "Divinity" (Uluhiyyah), because only He is worthy of worship who, in addition to "Lordship," has the power to punish, and in addition to the power to punish, His doors of mercy and forgiveness are also open.

Then, in a short and jolting sentence addressed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), He says: "Say: This is a great news" (قُلْ هُوَ نَبَأٌ عَظِيمٌ).

"From which you are turning away" (أَنْتُمْ عَنْهُ مُعْرِضُونَ).

What is this news that He refers to and describes with greatness? Is it the Holy Quran? The mission of the Prophet? The Resurrection and the fate of the believers and disbelievers? The Oneness and Unity of God? Or all of these?

Since the Quran encompasses all these matters and is the common link between them, and the turning away of the polytheists was from it, the most appropriate meaning is the first one, namely the Quran.

Yes, this great Heavenly Book is a great news, as great as the entire realm of existence, which has been revealed from the Creator of this world—the Creator who is the All-Mighty, the All-Forgiving, the One, and the Subduer. It is a news whose greatness a large group did not perceive at the time of its revelation; some took it in mockery and ridicule, some called it magic, and some named it poetry. But it was not long before this "Great News" (Naba' Azim) revealed its inner reality, changed the course of human history, cast its shadow over the expanse of the world, and brought about a great and brilliant civilization in all fields. Interestingly, the announcement of this great news was made in this Meccan Surah, at a time when the Muslims were apparently in the utmost state of weakness and inability, and the doors of victory and salvation seemed closed to them.

Even today, the greatness of this great news is not fully clear to the people of the world, or even to the Muslims themselves, and the future must reveal it. This statement of the Quran that "you are turning away from it" is still true, and it is this very turning away of the Muslims that has caused them to be unable to fully quench their thirst from this overflowing spring of Divine grace, to charge forward in the light of its rays, and to conquer the peaks of pride and honor.

Then, as a prelude to mentioning the story of the creation of Adam and the high value of human existence—to the extent that the angels all prostrated before him—He says:

"I had no knowledge of the Exalted Assembly (the angels of the higher world) when they were disputing [about the creation of man]" (ما كانَ لِي مِنْ عِلْمٍ بِالْمَلَإِ الْأَعْلى‌ إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ).

My awareness is only through revelation, and "nothing is revealed to me except that I am a clear warner" (إِنْ يُوحى‌ إِلَيَّ إِلَّا أَنَّما أَنَا نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ). Although the angels did not have a dispute or conflict with the Lord, the mere fact that when Allah addressed them saying, "I am going to create a vicegerent on earth," they engaged in a dialogue and said, "Will You place therein one who will cause corruption therein and shed blood?" to which He replied, "I know that which you do not know" (Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 30)—these dialogues are referred to as "disputing" (mukhasamah), which is a metaphorical usage. As we mentioned, this is in fact a prelude to the subsequent verses that speak about the creation of Adam.

There is also the possibility that "the Exalted Assembly" (al-Mala' al-A'la) has a broad meaning that even includes Iblis (Satan), since on that day Iblis was among the ranks of the angels and rose to dispute with God and opened his mouth in protest, and for this reason, he was eternally banished from the Divine presence; however, the first interpretation is more appropriate.

In several narrations reported through both Shi'a and Sunni sources, we read that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) asked one of his companions:

"Do you know what the Exalted Assembly is disputing about?" (أتدري فيما يختصم الملأ الأعلى؟) He replied: "No." The Prophet said: "They disputed regarding the 'expiations' (kaffarat) and the 'ranks' (darajat). As for the expiations, they are: performing a thorough ablution (wudu) in the cold of winter, walking toward congregational prayers, and waiting for one prayer after another. As for the ranks, they are: spreading the greeting of peace (Salam), feeding the hungry, and praying at night while the people are asleep." [1]

Of course, it is not explicitly stated in this hadith that it refers to the interpretation of the above verse, even though the expressions are similar to the expressions of the verse under discussion. In any case, it is understood from this hadith that "disputing" here means only discussion and dialogue, not conflict and strife—discussion regarding the deeds of human beings and the actions that become the expiation for sins and increase the ranks of humans. Perhaps their discussion was about the number of deeds that are the source of these virtues, or in determining the extent and measure of the ranks resulting from these deeds. Thus, a third interpretation for the verse is obtained from this hadith, which is appropriate in some respects but does not have much harmony with the upcoming verses; as we said, it may refer to other dialogues of the angels, not those related to these verses.

It is also noteworthy that the Prophet's lack of knowledge means: "I do not know anything of these matters from myself; I only know that which is revealed to me through revelation."

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed; this same hadith is narrated in Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur through many chains from a group of the Prophet's companions, with some variations.

Ayahs

إِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِنِّى خَـٰلِقٌۢ بَشَرًا مِّن طِينٍ (٧١)
When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am about to create a human being out of clay. (71)
فَإِذَا سَوَّيْتُهُۥ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِى فَقَعُوا۟ لَهُۥ سَـٰجِدِينَ (٧٢)
So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My spirit, then fall down in prostration before him.’ (72)
فَسَجَدَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ (٧٣)
Thereat the angels prostrated, all of them together, (73)
إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ ٱسْتَكْبَرَ وَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٧٤)
but not Iblis; he acted arrogantly and he was one of the faithless. (74)
قَالَ يَـٰٓإِبْلِيسُ مَا مَنَعَكَ أَن تَسْجُدَ لِمَا خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَىَّ ۖ أَسْتَكْبَرْتَ أَمْ كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْعَالِينَ (٧٥)
He said, ‘O Iblis! What keeps you from prostrating before that which I have created with My [own] two hands? Are you arrogant, or are you one of the exalted ones?’ (75)
قَالَ أَنَا۠ خَيْرٌ مِّنْهُ ۖ خَلَقْتَنِى مِن نَّارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُۥ مِن طِينٍ (٧٦)
‘I am better than him,’ he said. ‘You created me from fire and You created him from clay.’ (76)
قَالَ فَٱخْرُجْ مِنْهَا فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ (٧٧)
He said, ‘Begone hence, for you are indeed an outcast, (77)
وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ لَعْنَتِىٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ (٧٨)
and indeed My curse will be on you till the Day of Retribution.’ (78)
قَالَ رَبِّ فَأَنظِرْنِىٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ (٧٩)
He said, ‘My Lord! Respite me till the day they will be resurrected.’ (79)
قَالَ فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنظَرِينَ (٨٠)
Said He, ‘You are indeed among the reprieved (80)
إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْوَقْتِ ٱلْمَعْلُومِ (٨١)
until the day of the known time.’ (81)
قَالَ فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٨٢)
He said, ‘By Your might, I will surely pervert them, (82)
إِلَّا عِبَادَكَ مِنْهُمُ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ (٨٣)
except Your exclusive servants among them.’ (83)

Commentary

He Acted Arrogantly and Was Banished from God's Presence!

As we have mentioned, these verses provide an explanation regarding the "dispute of the Exalted Assembly" and "Iblis," and the dialogue concerning the creation of "Adam." Overall, the purpose of recounting this story is, firstly, to remind human beings that their existence is so valuable that all the angels fell prostrate before their forefather, Adam. How can a human being with such stature become a captive in the claws of Satan and base desires? How can he abandon his existential value or prostrate before stone and wood?! Fundamentally, one of the effective methods of education is granting character to those being educated, or more accurately: reminding them of their high character and existential worth. It is in such conditions that a human feels that baseness and degradation are beneath his dignity and instinctively avoids them.

Secondly, the obstinacy of Satan and his pride, arrogance, and envy—which caused him to fall forever from the heights of glory and sink into the mire of malediction—can serve as a warning to all stubborn and arrogant individuals to take heed and abandon the way of Satan.

Thirdly, it informs of the existence of such a great enemy who has sworn to seduce human beings, so that everyone remains vigilant and does not fall into his trap.

The sum of these matters serves as a completion to the previous discussions.

In any case, in the first verse, He says: "Remember when your Lord said to the angels: 'I am creating a human being from clay'" (إِذْ قالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلائِكَةِ إِنِّي خالِقٌ بَشَراً مِنْ طِينٍ).

However, so that it is not imagined that the human dimension is solely the earthly dimension, He adds in the next verse: "So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My soul (a noble and distinguished soul that I have created), fall down before him in prostration" (فَإِذا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَ نَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي فَقَعُوا لَهُ ساجِدِينَ).

In this manner, the creation of man was completed; the "Soul of Allah" and "dark clay" were blended together, and a wondrous and unprecedented being—whose arcs of ascent and descent were both infinite—was created. A being with extraordinary potential, capable of being worthy of the station of "Vicegerent of Allah" (Khalifatullah), stepped into the arena of existence, "and at that moment, all the angels prostrated without exception" (فَسَجَدَ الْمَلائِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ). They deemed the Creator worthy of praise for "fashioning such a delightful form from water and clay!"

But "the only one who did not prostrate was Iblis; he acted arrogantly and rebelled, and for this reason, he fell from his magnificent station and was among the disbelievers" (إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ اسْتَكْبَرَ وَ كانَ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ). Indeed, the worst plague of the human soul is this very pride and arrogance, which casts dark veils of ignorance over his seeing eyes, deprives him of perceiving realities, compels him toward rebellion and defiance, and casts him out from the ranks of the believers—the rank of God's obedient servants—placing him in the ranks of the disbelievers, the rank of the insurgents and tyrants, just as it did to Iblis.

It was here that Iblis was called to account and questioned by Allah:

"He said: 'O Iblis! What prevented you from prostrating to that which I created with My two hands?'" (قالَ يا إِبْلِيسُ ما مَنَعَكَ أَنْ تَسْجُدَ لِما خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَيَ).

It is obvious that the expression "My two hands" (yadai) does not refer to physical hands, as He is pure and sanctified from any form of body or physicality. Rather, "hand" here is a metaphor for power, because humans usually exercise their power with their hands. Therefore, in everyday expressions, this word is frequently used to mean power; it is said "such a country is in the hands of such a group," or "such a great temple or building was built by the hand of so-and-so." Sometimes it is said "my hand is short" (I am powerless) or "your hand is full" (you are capable).

In none of these usages does "hand" mean the specific limb; rather, all of them are metaphors for power and authority.

And since humans perform important tasks with both hands, and employing both hands is a sign of a person's ultimate attention and interest in something, the mention of this expression in the above verse is a metaphor for the special grace of the Lord and the exercise of His absolute power in the creation of man.

Then He adds: "Did you act arrogantly, or were you [already] among the haughty ones [who think they are above the command to prostrate]?" (أَسْتَكْبَرْتَ أَمْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الْعالِينَ).

Undoubtedly, no one can claim that their status and rank are above prostrating to Allah (or to Adam by Allah's command); therefore, the only remaining path is the second possibility, which is arrogance.

Some commentators interpret "Alin" (the haughty) here to mean those who always walk the path of pride and arrogance; thus, the meaning of the sentence becomes: "Have you acted arrogantly just now, or have you always been this way?" However, the first meaning seems more appropriate.

But Iblis, with ultimate audacity, chose the second option, believing he was superior to being given such a command. Thus, with total insolence, he attempted to argue against Allah's command and "said: 'I am better than him (Adam); You created me from fire and created him from clay!'" (قالَ أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْهُ خَلَقْتَنِي مِنْ نارٍ وَ خَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ طِينٍ).

In reality, he wanted to negate the Lord's command with three premises of his own imagining:

First, that "I am created from fire and he is from clay," which was a fact, as the Holy Quran states: "He created man from dry clay like pottery, and created the jinn (of whom Iblis is one) from a smokeless flame of fire" (Ar-Rahman 14-15).

The second premise was that what is created from fire is superior to what is created from earth, because fire is more noble than earth.

The third premise was that a superior being should never be commanded to prostrate before a non-superior being!! And all of Iblis's error lay in these last two premises.

Because, firstly, Adam was not made of clay alone; rather, his greatness came from that Divine Spirit breathed into him—otherwise, where is clay and where is all this glory, potential, and perfection?! Secondly: earth is not only not inferior to fire, but it is far superior to it. All life, vitality, and vital resources arise from the earth; plants, flowers, and all living creatures draw support from the earth; all precious minerals are hidden in the heart of the earth; in short, earth is the source of all kinds of blessings. Meanwhile, fire, despite all its importance in life, can never match it and is merely a tool for utilizing earthly resources—and a dangerous, destructive tool at that. Furthermore, combustible materials are mostly produced by the grace of the earth (wood, coal, oil, and the like).

Thirdly: the issue is one of obeying the Lord's command and carrying out His orders. All are His creatures and servants and must submit to His command.

In any case, if we dissect Iblis's reasoning, it leads to an extraordinary disbelief (kufr). With this statement, he wanted to negate both Allah's wisdom and (God forbid) consider His command baseless. This stance is evidence of his ultimate ignorance; for if he had said: "My base desires prevented me," or "pride and arrogance did not allow me," and the like, he would have only confessed to a sin. But now that he attempts to justify his rebellion by negating the Lord's wisdom and knowledge, it shows he has fallen to the lowest stage of disbelief.

Furthermore, a creature has no independence before the Creator; whatever it has is from Him. Iblis's tone shows that he claimed sovereignty and independence for himself against the sovereignty of the Lord, and this is another source of disbelief.

In any case, the factor of Satan's misguidance was a concoction of "selfishness," "pride," "arrogance," "ignorance," and "envy." These satanic traits joined hands and brought him down from that height of glory—he who for long years was a companion to the angels, indeed their teacher. How dangerous are these ugly traits wherever they are found?! As Ali عليه السلام stated in one of the sermons of "Nahj al-Balagha": "He had worshipped the Lord for thousands of years, but one hour of arrogance set all of it ablaze and scattered it to the wind!" [^1] Indeed, a grand and magnificent building must be constructed over many years, but it can be destroyed in a single moment with one powerful bomb!

It was here that this foul being had to be expelled from the ranks of the Exalted Assembly and the angels of the upper world. Therefore, Allah addressed him and "said: 'Get out of it (the ranks of the angels/the heavens), for you are rejected!'" (قالَ فَاخْرُجْ مِنْها فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ).

The pronoun in "fakhruj minha" (get out of it) may refer to the ranks of the angels, the upper worlds, Paradise, or the mercy of Allah.

Yes, this "stranger" must leave this place, for it is no longer his home. This is the place for the pure and the near ones, not for the polluted, the rebellious, and the dark-hearted.

"Rajim" is from the root "rajm," meaning to stone, and since stoning necessitates expulsion, it is sometimes used in this sense.

And then He added: "And surely My curse shall be upon you until the Day of Judgment" (وَ إِنَّ عَلَيْكَ لَعْنَتِي إِلى‌ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ‌). You shall always be banished from My mercy.

The important thing is that when a person reaps a grim result from their ugly deeds, they should wake up and think of making amends. But nothing is more dangerous than continuing to ride the mount of pride and obstinacy and proceeding on the path toward the precipice. It is here that, moment by moment, one's distance from the Straight Path increases, and this was the grim fate that befell "Iblis."

It was here that "envy" turned into "malice"—a harsh and deep-rooted malice. As the Quran says:

"He said: 'My Lord! Then give me respite until the Day they are resurrected'" (قالَ رَبِّ فَأَنْظِرْنِي إِلى‌ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ).

A respite to shed tears of regret and remorse over my past? A respite to compensate for my ugly and grim rebellion? No! A respite to take revenge on the children of Adam and lead them all astray—even though the misguidance of every single one of them places a new heavy burden of sin upon my shoulders and sinks me deeper into this mire of disbelief and rebellion! Oh, what disasters obstinacy, pride, arrogance, and envy bring upon individuals!! In truth, he wanted to spend every possible last moment seducing the children of Adam, because the Day of Resurrection is the end of the period of obligation (taklif), and temptation and seduction no longer have meaning. Furthermore, with this request, he sought to ward off death from himself and remain alive until the Resurrection, even if all the inhabitants of the world were to pass away.

Here, the Divine Will, for reasons we will mention later, necessitated that this request of Iblis be granted, but not absolutely—rather, conditionally. As He says in the next verse: "He said: 'Indeed, you are of those given respite'" (قالَ فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ الْمُنْظَرِينَ).

But not until the Day of Resurrection and the raising of the creatures, rather "until the Day of the Appointed Time" (إِلى‌ يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ).

Regarding what "the Day of the Appointed Time" is, commentators have various interpretations:

Some consider it the end of this world, because on that day all living beings die, and only the Pure Essence of Allah remains, as we read in verse 88 of Surah Al-Qasas: "Everything is perishing except His Face." In this way, part of Iblis's request was granted.

Some have suggested it refers to the Day of Judgment, but this possibility neither fits the apparent meaning of the verses under discussion—whose tone shows that his entire request was not granted—nor other verses of the Quran that inform of the death of all living beings at the end of this world.

There is also the possibility that the above verse refers to a time that no one knows except Allah.

However, the first interpretation is the most appropriate of all. Therefore, in a narration cited in Tafsir al-Burhan from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated that Iblis dies between the first and second trumpet blasts. [^1]

It was here that Iblis revealed what was hidden in his heart and showed his ultimate goal in requesting eternal life, and "said: 'By Your Might, I will surely mislead them all!'" (قالَ فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ).

Swearing by "Might" (Izzat) is to rely on power and demonstrate capability. These successive emphases (the oath on one hand, the heavy 'nun' of emphasis on the other, and the word "all" on the third) show that he had and has the ultimate persistence in his decision and stands by his word until his last breath.

However, he was aware of the reality that a group of Allah's special servants would not, at any price, fall into his sphere of influence and the domain of his temptation. Therefore, he was forced to exclude them from the above statement and said: "Except Your chosen servants among them" (إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ).

Those who walk the path of Your gnosis and servitude with sincerity, truthfulness, and purity; You have accepted them, purified them, and placed them in Your sphere of protection. It is only this group that I have no access to; otherwise, I will cast the rest into my trap! Incidentally, this conjecture of Iblis turned out to be correct, and everyone was caught in his trap in some way, and none were saved except the "sincere ones" (mukhlasin), just as the Quran says in verse 20 of Surah Saba: "And Iblis certainly found his assumption about them to be true, so they followed him, except for a party of the believers."

Key Points

1- The Philosophy of Satan's Existence

Regarding the above verses, important issues are raised, including: the matter of Satan's creation, the reason for the angels' prostration to Adam, the cause of Adam's superiority over the angels, whom Satan gains mastery over, the result of pride, arrogance, and selfishness, the meaning of "dark clay" and the "Soul of Allah," the matter of Adam's creation and his independent creation versus the hypothesis of the evolution of species, and other such issues which we have discussed in detail in Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 34 of Surah Al-Baqarah, Volume 11 under verse 26 of Surah Al-Hijr (page 74 onwards), and Volume 6 under verse 11 of Surah Al-A'raf (page 98 onwards).

What we find necessary to remind here again is first the question asked about the philosophy of Satan's creation.

Many ask: if man was created for perfection and to attain happiness through the servitude of Allah, what reason can there be for the existence of Satan—a destructive, anti-evolutionary being? And a being that is vigilant, malicious, cunning, deceptive, and determined! But if we reflect a little, we will know that the existence of this enemy is also an aid to the progress of human perfection.

Let us not look far; resistant forces always gain strength in the face of fierce enemies and traverse their evolutionary path.

The most skilled and powerful commanders and soldiers are those who have been engaged in great wars with fierce enemies.

Experienced and powerful politicians are those who have grappled with powerful enemies in the harsh furnaces of political crises.

Great wrestling champions are those who have tested their strength against powerful and stubborn opponents.

Therefore, what surprise is it that the great servants of Allah, through continuous and persistent struggle against "Satan," become stronger and more powerful day by day! Today's scientists say regarding the philosophy of the existence of intrusive microbes:

If they did not exist, the cells of the human body would fall into a state of lethargy and numbness, and human growth would likely not exceed 80 centimeters; everyone would be like midgets. Thus, modern humans have gained more strength and growth through physical struggle with intrusive microbes.

And such is the human soul in the struggle against Satan and base desires.

But this does not mean that Satan has a duty to seduce Allah's servants. From the first day, Satan had a pure creation, like all other creatures. Deviation, degradation, misery, and devilry came to him by his own will and choice. Therefore, Allah did not create Iblis as a devil from the first day; he himself chose to be a devil. Yet, at the same time, his devilry not only does not harm truth-seeking servants but is a ladder for their elevation (reflect on this).

However, the question remains: why did He accept his request regarding the continuation of his life, and why did He not destroy him immediately?! The answer to this question is what was mentioned above, or in other words:

The world is a field of trial and examination (a trial that is a means of nurturing and perfecting humans). And we know that testing is not possible except in the face of fierce enemies, storms, and crises.

Of course, even if there were no Satan, base desires and internal temptations would put man to the test, but with the existence of Satan, this furnace of testing became hotter, because Satan is an external factor and base desire is an internal factor!

2- The Fire of Pride and Arrogance Burns the Capital of Existence

Among the extremely sensitive issues in the story of "Iblis" and his banishment from Allah's presence that attracts attention is the impact of the factor of selfishness and pride in the fall and misery of man, such that it can be said: the most important and dangerous factor of deviation is this very factor.

It was this that brought six thousand years of worship to destruction in a single moment, and it was this that demoted a being who was in the ranks of the great angels of heaven to the lowest depths of wretchedness and made him deserving of Allah's eternal curse.

Selfishness and pride do not allow a person to see the face of truth as it is.

Selfishness is the source of envy, and envy is the source of malice, and malice is the factor of bloodshed and other crimes.

Selfishness compels man to continue in error and mistake, and in the presence of awakening factors, it neutralizes them all.

Selfishness and obstinacy take away the opportunity for repentance and compensation from man and close the doors of salvation to him. In short, whatever is said about the danger of this ugly and blameworthy trait is not enough.

And how beautifully the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, said:

"So take heed from what Allah did to Iblis, as He nullified his long deeds and great efforts—while he had worshipped Allah for six thousand years... because of one hour of arrogance. So who after Iblis can remain safe from Allah by committing a sin like his?"

"This enemy of Allah, Satan, is the leader of the fanatics and the predecessor of the arrogant, who laid the foundation of fanaticism, and contested with Allah for the robe of Grandeur, and donned the garment of self-exaltation, and cast off the veil of humility. Do you not see how Allah belittled him for his arrogance? And lowered him for his self-elevation? So He made him banished in this world and prepared for him the blazing fire in the Hereafter!" (Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 192, the Sermon of Disparagement/al-Qasi'ah).

Ayahs

قَالَ فَٱلْحَقُّ وَٱلْحَقَّ أَقُولُ (٨٤)
Said He, ‘The truth is that—and I speak the truth— (84)
لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنكَ وَمِمَّن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٨٥)
I will surely fill hell with you and all those who follow you.’ (85)
قُلْ مَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ (٨٦)
Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it, and I am no impostor. (86)
إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٨٧)
It is just a reminder for all the nations, (87)
وَلَتَعْلَمُنَّ نَبَأَهُۥ بَعْدَ حِينٍۭ (٨٨)
and you will surely learn its tidings in due time.’ (88)

Commentary

The Final Word Regarding Iblis!

These verses, which are the concluding verses of Surah Sad, are in fact a summary of the entire content of this Surah and a conclusion to the various discussions presented within it.

First, in response to "Iblis" who threatened to seduce all human beings except for the "sincere ones" (mukhlasin), Allah "said: By the Truth, and the Truth I speak" (قَالَ فَالْحَقُّ وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ) [1]

"That I will surely fill Hell with you and those of them who follow you, all together" (لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنْكَ وَمِمَّنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ). Everything from the beginning of the Surah until this point has been the Truth. That for which the great Prophets—glimpses of whose lives were mentioned in this Surah—struggled and fought was the Truth. The discourse regarding the Resurrection, the painful punishment of the rebellious, and the various blessings for the people of Paradise mentioned in this Surah were all the Truth. The end of the Surah is also the Truth, and Allah swears by the Truth and speaks the Truth that He will fill Hell with Satan and his followers, so that He may provide a decisive response to Iblis's statement regarding the seduction of humans, which was uttered with such certainty, and clarify the fate of everyone.

In any case, these two sentences contain numerous emphases: the issue of "Truth" (al-Haqq) is emphasized twice and sworn upon; the phrase "I will surely fill" (la-amla'anna) is accompanied by the heavy nun of emphasis; and "all together" (ajma'in) is a further reinforcement of all these, so that no one may harbor the slightest doubt or hesitation that there is no way of salvation for Satan and his followers, and that continuing on their path leads to the Abode of Ruin.

[1]: There is much discussion regarding the syntax of this sentence. It is possible that "al-Haqq" is the subject (mubtada) and "my oath" (qasami), which is the predicate (khabar), is omitted. It is also possible that its predicate is "my word" (f-al-Haqqu qawli). There is also the possibility that it is the predicate of an omitted subject, estimated as (hadha huwa al-Haqq) "this is the Truth" or (ana al-Haqq) "I am the Truth."

Then, at the conclusion of this discourse, He points to four important matters in short and clear phrases:

In the first stage, He says: "Say: I do not ask of you any reward for it" (قُلْ مَا أَسْأَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ).

In this way, He puts an end to the excuses of the excuse-seekers and makes it clear: "I only seek your salvation and prosperity; I ask of you neither material nor spiritual reward—neither appreciation nor gratitude, neither status nor governance. My reward is only upon Allah," as explicitly stated in other verses of the Holy Quran, such as verse 47 of Surah Saba: "إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلَى اللَّهِ" (My reward is only upon Allah).

This itself is one of the proofs of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) truthfulness, for a false claimant raises his claim for greedy motives, and his greed will inevitably manifest through his words in some form.

In the second stage, He says: "And I am not of the pretenders (mutakallifin)" (وَمَا أَنَا مِنَ الْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ). My words are coupled with reason and logic, and there is no affectation or pretension in them. My expressions are clear and my speech is free from any ambiguity or complexity.

In reality, the first sentence speaks of the attributes of the caller, and the second sentence speaks of the nature of the call and its content; it is truly a case of "the sun has come as the proof of the sun."

In the third stage, He states the primary goal of this great call and the revelation of this Heavenly Book, saying: "This [Quran] is nothing but a reminder and an awakening for all the worlds" (إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِلْعَالَمِينَ). Yes, the important thing is for people to emerge from negligence and engage in reflection and thought.

For the path is clear, its signs are manifest, and within the human soul is a pure primordial nature (fitrah) that gives him direction and draws him toward the line of Monotheism and piety. The key is awakening, and this is the primary mission of the Prophets and the Heavenly Books.

This expression, the likes of which are not few in the Holy Quran, shows that the content of the Prophets' call in all stages is harmonious with the God-given nature, and these two move forward hand-in-hand.

And in the fourth and final stage, He threatens the opponents with a short and meaningful phrase, saying: "And you will surely know its news after a time!" (وَلَتَعْلَمُنَّ نَبَأَهُ بَعْدَ حِينٍ). You may not take these words seriously and may pass by them with indifference, but soon the truth of my speech will become apparent—both in this world on the battlefields of Islam and disbelief, in the spheres of social and intellectual influence, and through Divine punishments; and also in the other world, where you will see the painful punishments of God. In short, whatever I have told you, you will witness with your own eyes in due time. In brief, the Divine whip is ready and will soon descend upon the backs of the arrogant and the oppressors.

Key Points Who is a Mutakallif (Pretender)?

In the above verses, we read that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) counts it as one of his honors that he is not among the mutakallifin (those who affect pretension or take upon themselves what is not theirs).

In Islamic narrations, there are many discussions regarding the signs and characteristics of the mutakallifin:

In a hadith narrated in Jawami' al-Jami' from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read: "The mutakallif has three signs: he disputes with those who are above him, he pursues what he can never attain, and he speaks of that which he does not know!" [1] The same meaning has been narrated with different wording from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) quoting the words of Luqman the Wise.

In another hadith from the testaments of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to Ali (عليه السلام), we read: "The mutakallif has three signs: he flatters when present, he backbites when absent, and he gloats at [another's] misfortune." [2]

Again, in another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read: "The mutakallif is in error even if he happens to be correct.

[1]: Jawami' al-Jami', as cited in Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 17, p. 243. [2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 473.

The mutakallif will gain nothing in the end but meanness and humiliation, and in the present, nothing but toil, hardship, and misery. The outward of the mutakallif is ostentation (riya) and his inward is hypocrisy (nifaq), and he constantly flies with these two wings! In short, affectation (takalluf) is not among the ethics of the righteous, nor is it the hallmark of the pious in any matter, just as Allah said to His Prophet: 'Say: I do not ask of you any reward and I am not of the pretenders.'" [1]

From the sum of these narrations, it is well understood that the mutakallifin are those who have stepped outside the path of truth, justice, honesty, and integrity. They ignore realities and turn to fantasies; they report on matters they have no knowledge of and interfere in things they do not understand. Their outward and inward are different, and their presence and absence are contradictory. They throw themselves into toil and hardship and achieve nothing but disgrace and misery. The God-fearing and the righteous are completely pure and free from this "trait."

O Lord! Grant us the success to drive away from ourselves all traces of affectation, hypocrisy, rebellion, and defiance. O Allah! Place us among the sincere ones (mukhlasin) whom You keep under Your protection and whom the seductive Satan despairs of reaching. O God! Bestow upon us that awakening and alertness to rise for the revival of the content of this great Quran, to mobilize the forces of Muslims throughout the world,

[1]: Same source.

to step in Your path with one heart and one voice, and to crush the enemies of truth and reality. Amin, O Lord of the Worlds.
